1.title: A MORNING LIKE EVERY OTHER
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Dawn*

The bell has not rung yet. You wake before it — always have, which is one of the small unglamorous disciplines that separate squires who want to be knights from squires who want to have been knights. The barracks smells of oiled leather and cold ash and the particular warmth of ten people sleeping in the same room in autumn. Aldric Voss sleeps across from you with his arms folded on his chest like a man who has already decided to be composed even in unconsciousness. Three bunks down, the squire who always trains alone — Vael Ashmore — has already made her bed with the tight corners of someone who left nothing undone, ever, and is gone. Eight months here. Seven days until the Trials. Outside the window the sky is the purple-grey of pre-dawn that looks almost exactly like the moment before something begins.

On your way past the narrow window you catch something: a figure on the armoury roof, just a silhouette against the last stars, sitting perfectly still with the patience of someone who has been there for hours. You do not know who it is. You file it away and keep moving.

The corridor notice board has a new posting, half-covered by the duty roster: *Press credential — Thessaly Brightwater, war correspondent, arrived three days prior. All personnel: cooperate within operational limits.* Someone has written underneath it in pencil: *She asked me what I was afraid of. I told her. Why did I tell her?*

Seven days. That is how many mornings remain before the formal Trials. You have been counting them differently lately — not as days remaining but as last-times. Last Tuesday at early formation. Last Wednesday in the chapel library with Aldous. This morning has the particular quality of the last first-morning-of-the-week before the thing that changes things. You get up before the bell anyway. You always do.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** Something is off this morning — the south-facing guards are doubled and there are fresh horses in the yard that were not there yesterday. New orders arrived overnight. You know before anyone tells you.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** The morning is ordinary. Nothing unusual is visible.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: The bell rings. Time to move.
Read the letter from home before the bell. Just once more.
The figure on the armoury roof. You want a better look before the day starts.

2.title: THE LETTER
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Barracks*

You have read it four times. The harvest fair, your father's knee worse in the cold, your sister grown two inches. That she is proud. That she is afraid. She does not say afraid of what. She does not need to. Your mother's handwriting slopes slightly left, which it always has. At the bottom, in different ink — she sealed it, then reopened it: 'Come home safe. Be good. Be the kind of knight they write songs about, not the kind they write tragedies about. I love you.' The second ink is darker. She wrote the rest of the letter first and then could not send it without saying the true thing.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** Reading between the lines: she knows something specific, not just general fear. Someone has been through your village recently. Someone official, heading south. This letter was written the day after they left.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** The worry in the letter is real but unreadable. You fold it and carry it.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Place it in your breast pocket. Then go train.
Set it down. Go practise. Sentiment waits.
Write back immediately, while the feeling is fresh.

3.title: ALDRIC IN THE COURTYARD
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Training Yard, Dawn*

He is already there, which is not surprising. Aldric Voss does not oversleep. He runs sword forms in the grey pre-dawn with the absorbed focus of someone not thinking about anything else — which is precisely what being very good at something looks like. He finishes his combination before he stops, because Aldric Voss does not interrupt things that should be finished. Then a slight smile that is not quite a smile. A nod toward the practice sword on the rack.

Aldric Voss is tall and fair, with the kind of face that defaults to a neutrality so complete it reads as severity until you learn to look for the small tells — the slight relaxation around the eyes when something amuses him, the almost imperceptible tightening when something doesn't. He has the build of someone who has been training since he could hold a sword and the posture of someone who has never been entirely at ease anywhere except in motion.

The yard smells of wet stone and the specific iron tang of weapons that have been out since before dawn. Eight months ago you would have hesitated before picking up the practice sword. You pick it up without thinking, which tells you something. Aldric settles into his guard and tilts his head slightly — the same adjustment every time, left side, the tell you found in the second month and have never mentioned. You found it by watching. He knows you found it. He uses it anyway, which is either trust or a counter-trap. You have not yet decided which.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION JOINS: ALDRIC VOSS**
Knight-squire, rival, possible love interest. Joins as sparring partner from this scene.
Role: Combat ally: +1 to your attack rolls when fighting side by side.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Perception (+1) | WIS | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** You spot a gap in his left-side footwork. Carry into Scene 12: spar check at Advantage.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** His form is tight this morning. Scene 12 is a fair fight.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**OPTIONAL SCENE — SERA NIGHTHOLLOW**
Before the spar: there is someone on the armoury roof — the same figure you noticed from the barracks window in the dark. She has not moved. She has been watching. If you want to find out who she is, turn to Scene 3B (The Armoury Roof) now. Scene 3B is her first appearance — no prior relationship needed. 3B leads directly to Scene 12 (the spar).

choices.text: Take the sword and spar.
Watch him first. Point out the gap.
Challenge him to a running race.
Tell him honestly you could not sleep. Ask if he is nervous.
Meet the woman on the armoury roof (Scene 3B — Sera Nighthollow)

4.title: THE MEASURE
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Chapel Library*

The chapel library is cold and smells of old parchment and tallow and three centuries of people asking difficult questions in a small room. The candles are thin — the chapel gets last priority for supplies, which says something about how equipment maintenance ranks relative to justice. You find the passage: Est Sularus oth Mithas — My honour is my life. Three words. Commentaries running to thousands of pages.

An older volume catches your eye — its margin contains annotations in an ancient script. [LANG:Istaran|*The script is unfamiliar, though the letters resemble old Common.*]'Ante-Cataclysmus, the knights wrote this oath in blood. The Kingpriest called it heresy. They kept it anyway. — Loremaster Vareth, Temple of Istar, 13 PC.'[/LANG] The book is three hundred years old, from before the gods fell silent.

You are reading one of them when Ser Aldous sits down without being invited, sets his elbows on the table, looks at you, and says: 'Ask it.' You did not know there was a question visible on your face.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Religion or History (INT, proficiency if trained) (+0) | INT | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** You find the commentary passage that explains what Est Sularus actually meant when first written — not abstract honour, but a specific refusal of a corrupt command three centuries ago. The words have teeth. When Aldous asks his question you have the context to answer it well. +1 to your response in this scene.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** The passage is clear but the history behind it is opaque. You know the words. You do not yet fully know what they cost.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: 'What do you do when the law asks you to be cruel?'
'Is the Measure still true if the world it was written for is gone?'
'I am not sure I am good enough for the Trials.'
Say nothing. Close the book. Head to the courtyard.

5.title: THE FIRST EVALUATION
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Assessment Hall*

Lord Caeron Ashvale conducts the morning evaluation with the economy of a man who has done this a hundred times and sees no reason to approach it differently on the hundred-and-first. He works through the squire line without warmth or hurry, marking his clipboard in the small precise hand you can identify from across a courtyard. When he reaches you, he takes your sword. He examines the blade — clean, sharp, well-maintained — and then he finds the pommel dent. He holds it at eye level and examines the dent with the expression of a man not looking at the dent but at the choice that left it there. His verdict: 'Satisfactory.' The word lands like a door being closed by someone very good at closing doors quietly.

Lord Caeron Ashvale is thirty, and already carries the compressed quality of a man who does not waste movement or word. Dark hair, pale eyes that take inventory before they settle. The armour shows years of use — bought before he could afford to replace it, maintained since out of something other than necessity.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL SCENE — VAEL ASHMORE**
You are not the only squire waiting for Caeron's results. The one who always trains alone — the empty bunk from the barracks, tight corners, already gone before dawn — is leaning against the corridor wall three paces away, holding a sword with an identical pommel dent. If you want to meet Vael Ashmore, turn to Scene 5B (The Same Dent) now. Scene 5B is her first appearance — she is in this same corridor. Read 5B instead of this scene. 5B leads to Scene 11.

**COMPANION JOINS: LORD CAERON ASHVALE**
Your evaluating officer. Cold, exacting, possible love interest.
Role: Quest-giver for formal assignments. His approval unlocks Crown path bonuses.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (+1) | WIS | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** This is a test of your reaction, not your sword. +1 to any Caeron Persuasion check this adventure.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** He is completely unreadable.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Meet his gaze and accept the verdict.
Apologise for the dent and ask for the correct protocol.
'I can do better, my lord.'
Ask whether satisfactory is the highest mark given today.
Meet the other squire waiting for results (Scene 5B — Vael Ashmore)

6.title: THE CORRESPONDENT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — East Gate*

You cross the courtyard at a walk that is not quite a walk — the morning air is sharp and the castle is stirring around you. On the way, one of the older squires — Darric, the one who never gossips but always somehow knows things — catches your arm. 'That correspondent,' he says, low. 'Brightwater. She asked me yesterday what I would die for. I told her. I have no idea why I told her.' He lets go. 'Watch yourself at the east gate.' He says it like advice, not warning.
The east gate is quieter than the yard, the kind of place where people pass through rather than linger. Someone is lingering.

You have seen her around the garrison for three days now — ink-stained fingers, a coat with too many pockets, the steady pace of someone working through a list of people she intends to understand. She interviewed the senior knights on the first day, both stable hands on the second, and apparently got Zinnia Quickfingers talking for an hour, which is either a miracle or an interrogation technique. The notice board posting said war correspondent. The pencilled note underneath said something less official.

Thessaly Brightwater catches you at the east gate with her notebook open and her pen uncapped and the particular quality of attention that does not permit comfortable evasion. 'Tell me something true,' she says, 'about why you are here.' Not what you believe. Not what you want. Something true.

Thessaly Brightwater is half-elven, which shows mostly in the pointed ears and the quality of attention she gives things. Dark hair cut short and practical, ink on her fingers, a coat with too many pockets, all in use. She looks like someone for whom the road has become the natural state.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION JOINS: THESSALY BRIGHTWATER**
War correspondent. She hears things. She will share them if she trusts you.
Role: Intelligence and information network. +1 to Scene 23 and 56 if she trusts you.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** She has asked this question before — you can see it in how her pen is already positioned. She is not writing down the answer yet. She is waiting for something specific: the moment a person stops reciting and starts speaking. Answer honestly and she will mark the moment exactly.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** Her expression is professionally neutral. You cannot tell what she is looking for.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: 'Because I watched the south burn and I need to be able to do something.'
'Because my family has been Solamnic for six generations.'
'Because the Measure might be the last thing standing between Ansalon and darkness.'
'I am not sure yet. Is that honest enough?'

7.title: AN HONEST MOMENT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Training Yard*

You ask because the yard is empty and the day has not started yet and you have been wondering about this for eight months and you may not get another unguarded moment. He is quiet. Not the quiet of no answer — the quiet of deciding whether to give it. Then: 'Every morning.' He does not look at you. 'I just do not talk about it.' He keeps looking at the courtyard wall as if it is very interesting. This is the most honest thing he has said to you at six in the morning, which means it is probably the most honest thing he has said to anyone at six in the morning, including himself.

The day has not started yet. The bell will ring in eight minutes and then it will have started and this — whatever this is — will become part of the past. He is still looking at the wall. You let him. You understand that he gave you something just now that cost him something, in the way it always costs something when a person who is very good at being composed lets you see underneath it for a moment. You do not remark on it. That is also something.

boxes.text:
**ROMANCE MOMENT**
There is a pause before he turns back to the sword forms. The quality of the silence is different now.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** He is not just saying what sounds right — he has been awake since before you. His sword forms are running faster than usual, burning off something. Aldric Voss is genuinely frightened of failing the Trials. ROMANCE SPARK Aldric at Advantage if you respond to the fear rather than the bravado.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** He says it easily. You cannot tell if it is practiced or true.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Tell him you are nervous too.
'Then we will both pass anyway.'

8.title: SER ALDOUS ON JUSTICE
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Chapel Library*

He is quiet long enough that you wonder if he will answer. Then: 'The Measure says the law exists to protect the innocent.' He sets his commentary text down. He is not reading from it. 'If the law stops doing that — if a decree comes down requiring you to act against an innocent — the Measure itself gives you the answer. Est Sularus oth Mithas. My honour is my life.' He looks at you with the steadiness of someone who arrived at this through experience rather than reading. 'Not your lord's honour. Not the Order's. Yours. You carry it. You are responsible for where it goes.'

Ser Aldous is seventy or near it, with white hair, a weathered face, and the unhurried physicality of someone who was once very strong and is still, in the ways that matter, exactly that. He wears the chaplain's robe over the frame of a man who spent forty years in armour before he took it off, and he gives the impression of someone who removed the armour but kept everything it stood for.

boxes.text:
**You Gain:**
Ser Aldous as Mentor (+1 to ROSE checks in Act One). The Rose Commentary text — relevant in Scene 31.

**FIGHTER — MEASURE SUBPLOT**
Before your choice below, Fighters turn to Scene M1 (The Letter and the Spirit) now. It occurs this same morning. M1 leads back to Scene 9 after your choice there.

**PALADIN — THE HEALER'S TENT**
Before your choice below, Paladins may also visit the infirmary. Turn to Scene 9 (The Healer's Tent) now. It occurs this same morning — Aldous sends you. 'There is someone in the infirmary who could use a steady hand,' he says. 'Go. Come back when you have something to tell me about mercy.' Scene 9 leads back here for your choice.

choices.text: Thank him and read the text immediately.
'Have you ever had to make that choice yourself?'
Thank him and go to the infirmary. 'There is someone who could use a steady hand.'
'Have you ever had to make that choice yourself?' Then go where he sends you — the infirmary.

9.title: THE HEALER'S TENT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Infirmary*

The corridor from the east wing is long enough for the conversation to settle into something you carry rather than something you are still having. By the time you reach the infirmary door, the morning has moved on.

The infirmary smells of yarrow and clean linen and underneath both, the copper-salt note of pain being handled by someone who knows exactly what they are doing. Mira of the Oaks has not slept — you can see it in the particular stillness that develops past tiredness, when the body stops negotiating and runs on purpose. She does not look up when you come in. 'Sit with him.' She nods at the old man in the corner who is holding his own hands and staring at nothing with the look of someone who has been frightened long enough to have forgotten what unfrightened felt like. 'Hold his hand. Talk to him. He is frightened and I cannot be in two places at the same time.'

Mira of the Oaks is small and precise, with the steady hands of a healer and the look of someone who takes stock of a room before deciding how to be in it. Dark skin, dark eyes, hair pulled back in the functional style of someone who needs to see clearly and cannot have things in the way. She is younger than her competence suggests — mid-twenties, maybe — but the competence is entirely real.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION JOINS: MIRA OF THE OAKS**
Healer and refugee. Will travel with you if you earn her trust.
Role: Party healer: restores 1d6+2 HP to any companion once per scene.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Medicine (+1) | WIS | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** You recall a calming technique. The old man steadies. Mira notices. +1 Mira romance track.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** You do your best. No penalty — the kindness still counted.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

You kneel beside the old man and lay your hands on his arm. Warmth flows through your palms — not heat, not magic as you imagined it, but something quieter. A steadying. The old man's breathing eases. His grip on the cot rail loosens. This is the first time your power has worked so clearly. Something in you recognises Mishakal's touch. Mark 1 tally under MISHAKAL.

choices.text: Sit with the old man until Mira can take over.
Ask Mira how you can be most useful.
Apologise — you have training — and leave.
: Return to where you were. Carry what you learned here.

10.title: THE ADMISSION
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Chapel Steps*

Aldous laughs. Not the polite laugh of someone managing you — a genuine one, warm and immediate, with the deep lines around his eyes that come from a lifetime of doing it. 'Every squire worth their salt feels that,' he says. 'The ones who do not are the ones I worry about.' He settles back in his chair with the unhurried quality of someone who is exactly where he intended to be this morning. 'The question,' he says, 'is not whether you feel it. The question is what you do with it. Which brings us to what you are actually afraid of.' He waits. He is good at waiting. You get the sense he has had this conversation a great many times and has learned that the silence does more work than the prompting.

He sets down the commentary text he was pretending to read and looks at you with the specific attention of someone who has been waiting for this particular conversation since the first week you arrived. 'You are the kind of squire who asks whether they are good enough,' he says. 'That is a more interesting question than the ones most of your cohort are asking. The ones asking it are rarely the ones I end up worried about.'

choices.text: 'I am afraid I will do everything right and it will not be enough.'
'I am afraid I will make the wrong choice when it actually matters.'
'I am afraid I will not know the right choice until it is too late.'

11.title: THE DAY BEGINS
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Main Courtyard*

The bell rings at the same time it always does. Formation assembles in the main courtyard with the efficiency of people whose bodies have been doing this for eight months without consulting them. You find your place. Aldric finds his, three feet to your right, where he has been every morning for eight months.

**IF VAEL IS IN YOUR PARTY:** Vael is three positions to your left in formation. She does not look at you. She is looking at Caeron, with the same focused assessment you have seen her bring to her own sword dents — the attention of someone who is studying how a thing works so she can understand why it sometimes doesn't. After formation she catches your eye. A slight nod. Not casual. The nod of someone who has been paying attention and wants you to know it.

Lord Caeron reads out the day's assignments with the unhurried precision of a man who has already decided everything and is now informing you of the results. Field exercise. South road. Reported bandits. He reads out the pairs. Your name, then Aldric's. You feel Aldric absorb it without moving.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** History (INT, proficiency if trained) (+3) | INT | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** You recall a garrison briefing from three weeks ago: there have been refugee reports on the south road for over a month, not just the past few days. Someone suppressed those earlier reports. The 'bandits' may have been there much longer than the official timeline suggests.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** You have no specific knowledge of the south road beyond what Caeron just said.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**OPTIONAL SCENE — DRAGONLANCE CAMEO**
Before your preparation choice below: if you want a brief encounter with a canon character from the Dragonlance world, turn to Scene 11B now. It is non-mandatory flavour. Return here after 11B.

choices.text: Continue to Scene 12 — the spar.
A familiar face in the courtyard (Scene 11B — optional canon encounter)
Plan the exercise with Aldric together.
Find Thessaly first — she may have intelligence.
Train harder. You want to be sharp.
Check on Mira before you march.
Go to the mess hall. Eat. Watch. Learn.
Take a night watch shift. The wall has things to teach you.
Visit the forge. Your blade needs attention.
Check the infirmary for refugees.
Spend the evening getting to know your companions better.

12.title: THE SPAR
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Training Yard*

Twenty minutes in and you are evenly matched. This is not how this usually goes — Aldric Voss has been beating you in sparring for seven months by the specific margin of someone who is better and knows it and does not need to prove it further. Tonight the margin has disappeared. He pauses, breathing hard, and looks at you with an expression that takes a moment to identify because you have not seen it before: surprised. Not unhappy. Surprised.

'You have been watching me,' he says. Not an accusation. An assessment. He looks pleased in the way that people who take competition seriously look pleased when the competition is finally worth their attention. 'The tell. You found it.' You did. Three months ago. You said nothing. 'Good,' he says — and means it — and raises the practice sword again.

The spar is over. The morning bell rings. Formation assembles. Whatever happened between you and Aldric in the yard — the gap, the finding, the almost-laugh — is something you will both carry into the day without mentioning. That is also something.

boxes.text:
**ROLL REQUIRED:** Athletics (+3) | STR | DC 14
**SUCCESS (roll 14+):** You find the gap on his third combination. He goes down on one knee, laughing.. His respect is genuine now.
**FAILURE (roll 1-13):** He wins by a narrow margin.. 'Better than last month.'
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Let him win. Concede with grace. He hesitates — he knows what you did — and lowers his blade. 'Next time,' he says quietly, 'do not give me anything.' But there is something new in his voice. Respect, perhaps. Or something closer.
Take your opening. Win clean. He hits the ground hard. The yard goes silent. Then he laughs — actually laughs — and takes your offered hand. 'Finally,' he says. 'I was getting bored.'
Call it a draw. You both step back at the same moment. Neither of you says anything. Neither of you needs to. The morning bell rings and you walk to formation side by side.

13.title: THE REPAIRED SWORD
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury*

The armoury smells of iron filings and neatsfoot oil and the particular efficiency of a woman who has been doing this work for longer than you have been alive and views every preventable problem as a small personal insult. She takes your sword without comment, examines the pommel dent with the expression of someone who has seen worse but finds worse no consolation, and sets it in the repair queue. While you wait, Lord Caeron arrives for his monthly equipment review. He acknowledges you with the absence of acknowledgment that means he has registered you and is thinking about it.

He works his way through the rack with the same clipboard efficiency. When he reaches your sword — the dent now smoothed, the pommel flush and clean — he holds it at eye level for exactly the same amount of time as every other sword. He makes his mark. He sets it down. He moves to the next one. He does not comment. You understand, from everything you know about Lord Caeron Ashvale, that not commenting is the comment.

choices.text: 'Thank you, my lord.' Accept it quietly. He inclines his head — the smallest acknowledgment — and moves on. The silence says: *you are noted. You are not yet interesting.*
'What do you look for in a squire, my lord?'
'I notice you inspect your own equipment personally.'

14.title: I CAN DO BETTER
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Assessment Hall*

He does not look up. He makes a small mark on his clipboard. Then: 'Then do.' You turn to go. You are almost at the door when he adds — same tone, almost to himself but not quite: 'The dent is a small thing. It is the attention to small things that determines a knight's character.' He is already looking at the next item on his list. The assessment is over. The other thing hangs in the empty hall.

You are in the corridor when you fully understand what he said. Not the 'then do' — that was expected, brisk, over. The other thing. The one he said almost to himself. The dent is a small thing. It is the attention to small things. He is not talking about the sword. You replay it three times in the next twenty steps and by the third time you have decided it is the most useful thing anyone has said to you this week, possibly this month.

boxes.text:
**ROMANCE MOMENT**
He watches you leave. The pause before he returns to his papers is slightly longer than necessary.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 14
**SUCCESS (roll 14+):** He said it to your back because he cannot say it to your face — not yet. That addendum cost him something. He does not give unsolicited assessments. You are the first squire this year he has followed with an additional remark. ROMANCE SPARK Caeron activates at Advantage if you act on this knowledge.
**FAILURE (roll 1-13):** He speaks to the room. You are not sure if the remark was for you or habit.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Go directly to the armoury and fix the dent today.
Find Aldric — spar and work off the feeling.

15.title: THE POINTED QUESTION
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Assessment Hall*

'Satisfactory is not the highest mark given today,' he says. 'Ser Voss received the same. You and he are currently equal. That answer will be the last one that surprises me from you. I would advise you not to lose the instinct that generated it.'

He leaves before you can respond. The hall is briefly, completely quiet. The other squire who was waiting for their result has apparently vanished. You stand in the empty corridor with the feeling of someone who has just been told something important in a language they are still learning.

choices.text: File it away and move on. The corridor empties. You carry the words with you like a puzzle without an answer — yet.

Find Aldric and tell him. He will find it funny or annoying. 'Satisfactory,' he repeats, flat. Then the corner of his mouth twitches. 'We should celebrate. Identically.' It is almost a joke. From Aldric, this is progress.
Ask around about Lord Caeron. Who is he really?

16.title: WHAT THESSALY WRITES DOWN
prose: *Castle Brightholm — East Gate*
'That is the first honest answer I have gotten all week.' She asks if she can follow your progress through the Trials. In exchange, she will share what she hears.

There is something in how she frames it — not 'may I follow your progress' but a genuine exchange, an offer of her own access for yours. She has better intelligence than the garrison. You can see that from the map she carries and the ink still drying on the page she just turned. She has been here three days and she already knows things Lord Caeron does not. Whatever she is writing down, it is worth having her write it.

choices.text: Yes. An ally who hears everything is valuable. She smiles — quick and genuine — and makes a note. 'Then we have an arrangement.' Her pen is already moving again.
Yes, but you want to read what she writes first. She raises an eyebrow. 'Fair,' she says, and hands you the page. It is surprisingly accurate. Also surprisingly flattering.
You would rather she observed and formed her own conclusions.

17.title: THE FAMILY NAME
prose: *Castle Brightholm — East Gate*
'Six generations. Does that feel like a gift or a weight?'

It is the kind of question that sounds simple and isn't. Thessaly asks it the way she asks everything — without judgment already loaded into it, genuinely waiting to see what you say. Her pen is not moving. Whatever you answer, she is listening, not transcribing.

She found you near the east wall, which is not a coincidence. Thessaly Brightwater does not accidentally find people. The evening is cold and the torches have been lit and the garrison has the quieter particular quality of a place preparing for something. She has her notebook closed, which means this is not on the record, and her pen behind her ear, which means she might change her mind.

choices.text: 'Both. Always both.'
'A gift. I carry it proudly.'
'Mostly a weight lately.'

19.title: THE FIRM WORD
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Squire Quarters*
You stand in the restored quiet of your tent. The quill is back on the footlocker. Everything is exactly where it should be. You are not sure why this is unsatisfying.

The interaction with Zinnia — who borrowed your quill to write something she called 'extremely urgent correspondence' and returned it covered in blue ink — was brief, entirely reasonable on your part, and is now sitting oddly. You were polite. You were clear. She left without apparent offense. None of this explains the specific quality of the silence she left behind.

choices.text: Go after her and apologise — you were too stiff.
Let it go. She understood.

20.title: WHAT ALDOUS KNOWS
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Chapel Steps*

'A village. Twenty years ago. I had orders that were legal. That followed the Measure to the letter. And they were wrong. I followed them anyway because I told myself it was not my place to decide. The village is not there anymore.' He says it the way someone says a thing they have said before — out loud, to themselves, in the dark — enough times that the words are worn smooth. Not healed. Smooth.

He is quiet for a moment. The chapel library is cold and the candle between you is down to its last inch and neither of you moves to replace it. 'I tell you this,' he says finally, 'not because it is a cautionary tale. It is not a cautionary tale. It is a true story about what happens when you let the law do your thinking. The Measure exists to protect people. If you ever hold the Measure in one hand and a person in the other and you are not certain which one matters more — that uncertainty is the answer.' He closes the commentary text. The lesson is over. The weight of it is not.

boxes.text:
**You Gain:**
Aldous's Trust. Unlocks a critical path in Scene 31.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** He has told this story before — not often, but deliberately. He chose to tell it to you now and not six months ago. Something about how you asked made him think you were ready for it. He is giving you the most important thing he can: evidence that the Measure can become a shield if you let it. This is instruction, not confession.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** It is a heavy story told plainly. You receive it as such.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Thank him. Mean it. He meets your eyes — brief, direct — and nods once. The nod of a man who has just given you something he cannot take back.
Sit with him in silence a while longer. The candle gutters out. Neither of you moves to relight it. When you finally rise to leave, he says only: 'Tomorrow.' It is both a farewell and a command.

21.title: THE HEALER'S GIFT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Infirmary*

She prepared it before you asked — before the assignment was formally posted, even. Dried yarrow. Clean bandages rolled tight. A small clay jar of salve smelling of pine and something you cannot name. 'The yarrow slows bleeding. Press hard and do not move them until it stops. The salve is for infection — draconian wounds tend to close wrong without it.' She says this the way she says everything medical: factual, complete, without drama. Her hand is warm when yours brush taking the bundle. She does not pull away immediately.

boxes.text:
**ROMANCE MOMENT**
Her hand is warm when yours brush. 'Be careful,' she says — two words carrying more weight than their purpose.

**ADD TO INVENTORY — MIRA'S TRAVEL BUNDLE**
Mira prepared this before you asked. Native PC and CPC: add to your inventory or equipment list. • Dried yarrow (press hard: slows bleeding, DC 10 Medicine to apply) • Clean bandages (2 uses, each stabilises at 1 HP) • Pine salve (prevents infection in draconian wounds — use within 1 hour of injury)

choices.text: 'I will. Thank you.' Hold her gaze. She holds it back — longer than professional necessity requires. 'Be careful,' she says. Two words carrying more weight than their purpose.

Ask her to come as a field medic. 'I am needed here,' she says — but there is something in her voice that suggests she considered it. 'Come back in one piece. That is what I can do for the south road.'
Accept efficiently and leave. She watches you go — you feel it rather than see it. Healers are practical people. So are you.

22.title: ALDRIC'S PLAN
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Map Room*

He already has a plan. Aldric Voss does not arrive at planning sessions — he arrives to them. The approach routes are sketched in charcoal on the back of his exercise schedule with timing estimates and threat assessments in his small precise handwriting. 'Three approaches. Direct is fastest but visible from the ridge. East through the treeline is slower but quiet. West crosses the river and needs a ford.' He looks at you after each one. Not for permission. For what you think. This is what trust looks like when Aldric Voss demonstrates it.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** History (+3) | INT | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** The eastern treeline has a hidden ford — the route is faster than Aldric thinks.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** You have no useful knowledge of this terrain.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: 'East. Agreed.'
'Direct. We are knights, not scouts.'
'What is on the west bank? I want more information.'
'East — but split up. Meet at the ridge.'
'Wait. Let me check with Sera first — she has been watching the south road.'

23.title: THESSALY'S INTELLIGENCE
prose: *Castle Brightholm — East Gate*

Thessaly has a better map than the garrison does. Not better as in more detailed — better as in updated, with marks the garrison does not have, from sources the garrison has not consulted. She spreads it on the table in the field tent and shows you a red mark. 'Refugees,' she says. 'About thirty. Here, not here' — she taps the bandit location — 'here.' The second location is further south. 'The bandit report and the refugee report are from the same place. Draw your own conclusions.'

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Investigation (INT, proficiency if trained) (+3) | INT | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** Thessaly's map has three additional marks she did not explain. Cross-referencing with garrison road records: the Dragon Army has made at least two probing passes on this road in the last month. The refugees are not just fleeing the war — they are fleeing something that has been methodically clearing the area ahead of the main advance.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** The map shows what Thessaly told you. Nothing more is visible.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Take the map directly to Lord Caeron before the exercise.
Share it with Aldric and plan together.
Ask Thessaly to come with you as an observer.

24.title: SHARPENING THE BLADE
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Training Yard*

You train until the light fails. Running forms. Working the gap in your guard that you spotted in Scene 3 and have been quietly furious about ever since. The yard empties around you. The evening bell rings. You keep going.

There is a particular quality to training when no one is watching — no evaluation marks, no Caeron with his clipboard, no Aldric to compete with. Just you and the forms and the steady accumulation of marginally better. It is not exciting. It is the point. Aldric finds you packing up. 'You have improved,' he says. From Aldric Voss, this is equivalent to a sonnet.

choices.text: 'Yes.' You mean it. Aldric tilts his head — acknowledgment, assessment — and walks beside you back toward the barracks. The silence is companionable.
'Ask me at the end of tomorrow.' He almost smiles. Almost. 'That is the right answer,' he says. 'But I will remind you: the end of tomorrow is not a safe bet on a patrol.'

'I will not be ready until I am there.' He studies you the way he studies a sparring opponent — finding the weak points, deciding what to do about them. 'No one is,' he says finally. 'That is why we train.'

25.title: BEFORE THE MARCH
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Infirmary, Dawn*

She is awake before you come. The infirmary at pre-dawn has the quality of somewhere that has been working all night without anyone noticing. She hands you the folded paper before you speak — the yarrow directions in her careful hand. 'I prepared it this morning. Just in case.' She looks away when she says it. Not evasively — the way people look away when they are giving you something they cannot take back and they need a moment to finish releasing it. 'Be careful today.'

boxes.text:
**ROMANCE MOMENT**
You stand in the infirmary doorway with morning light behind you and Mira will not quite look at you and you will not quite leave.

choices.text: 'I will come back and tell you how it went.' She meets your eyes finally. 'Yes,' she says. 'You will.' Not a question. A requirement.
Nod. Say nothing. It means the same thing. She nods back — the nod of someone who has spent too many dawns watching people leave and knows better than to ask for words.
March south — the road ambush awaits (Scene 26)

29.title: CAERON'S ANSWER
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury*

He says it when he thinks you are not watching — which means he says it when he thinks the conversation is over and the thing he was holding back while it was not over can now be safely set down. 'I became a knight because being a lord meant staying behind walls when the bad things came.' A pause while he examines the hilt of his sword. 'I have not always been correct about what constitutes a bad thing. That is something I have had to learn at intervals. I prefer not to keep learning it.'

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 15
**SUCCESS (roll 15+):** He is describing a specific incident, not a general philosophy. There is a moment in his past where he made a command decision he still questions — not the same as Aldous's, but adjacent. If you note this and ask carefully in a later scene, he will tell you what it was.
**FAILURE (roll 1-14):** He is speaking generally. The history behind the words is not visible.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Leave quietly.
'I think you are more correct about it than you believe.'

30.title: WHO IS CAERON
prose: *Castle Brightholm*

Third son of a minor noble house, passed over for inheritance. Became a knight because there was nothing else and found out he was exceptional at it. His house considers knighthood beneath them. He considers them beneath the Measure.

What you piece together: the armour he wears is paid for out of his own salary, not the family estate. The saddle on his horse is older than most of the garrison squires. He does not mention his family. No one who knows him mentions it either — which tells you something about how that subject was closed. A man who chose this life at real cost, and will not let it be anything less than exactly what it should be.

choices.text: This makes you respect him differently.
This makes you understand him. There is a difference.

33.title: THE MESS HALL
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Midday*

The mess hall operates on a schedule that has not changed in forty years, which is either a testament to institutional consistency or evidence that no one with authority has eaten here recently. Long trestle tables, benches worn smooth, the smell of bread and something involving turnips that has been a source of garrison debate since before you arrived. The bread is good. The bread has always been good. The rest is a matter of opinion held by people who have stopped expecting otherwise.

Aldric eats like he does everything else — efficiently, without wasted motion, finishing before you are half done. Across the hall, Thessaly is interviewing one of the kitchen staff. Whatever she is learning about turnip procurement is, knowing Thessaly, somehow connected to garrison supply routes and by extension the Dragon Army's logistical capacity. Two tables down, Ser Aldous eats alone with a book open beside his plate and the comfortable air of a man who considers a meal without reading a meal wasted.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** At the corner table, partly hidden by the pillar: Lord Caeron is eating alone. He is not reading. He is watching the room — specifically, the squires. Specifically, for slightly longer than the others, you. When your eyes meet he looks down at his plate with the speed of someone who was not expecting to be caught. +1 to any Caeron Insight check this Act.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** The mess hall is busy and loud and you notice nothing beyond the turnips.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**NPC CROSS-TALK — ALDRIC AND THESSALY**
Aldric and Thessaly are arguing — not loudly, but with the focused energy of two people who are both certain they are correct. The subject, as far as you can tell: whether the garrison's intelligence on the south road is adequate. Thessaly thinks it is catastrophically insufficient. Aldric thinks the chain of command will provide. They are both, in different ways, right. Thessaly catches your eye mid-argument and raises one eyebrow — an invitation to weigh in, or permission to enjoy the show.

choices.text: Join the argument. Side with Thessaly — the intelligence is inadequate.
Join the argument. Side with Aldric — the chain of command exists for a reason.
Listen. Do not weigh in. Take the bread.
Sit with Aldous instead. You want to ask about something.

34.title: THE WATCH
prose: *Castle Brightholm — East Wall, Night*

The night watch is four hours of looking at darkness and learning what darkness sounds like when nothing is in it. The east wall faces the road and the road faces south and south is where, at some indeterminate distance, something is happening that has not reached you yet. The stars are Solinari, half-full, and whatever scattered light the lesser moons provide. The wall is cold stone and you lean against it and breathe and the air smells of pine and frost and the particular mineral quality of a late autumn night in northern Solamnia.
You are alone for the first hour. Then you are not.

**IF SERA IS IN YOUR PARTY:** Sera appears without announcement, as she does. She sits on the wall rather than leaning against it, legs dangling over the dark, and for ten minutes she says nothing. Then: 'Listen.' You listen. Wind. Pine. The creak of the gate timber. Nothing. 'Exactly,' she says. 'That is what worries me. No night birds. They stopped two days ago.' She looks at you with the flat professional gaze that means she is trusting you with something she has not told the senior officers yet. 'Something is on the south road. Something that frightened the wildlife before the patrols noticed.'

**IF SERA IS NOT IN YOUR PARTY:** You are alone for the four hours. This is not a hardship. There is a particular quality to standing watch that you have come to value — the suspension of the day's noise, the reduction to essentials: eyes, ears, the dark, the question of what is in it. You learn more about Castle Brightholm from its silence than from its bustle. The stone cools at a rate that tells you the season. The wind shifts at the second hour, carrying something from the south that is not quite smoke and not quite nothing.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** Just before the third hour: a light on the south road. Brief — a lantern uncovered and covered again, the signal pattern used by Solamnic scouts to report all-clear. But the garrison did not send a scout tonight. Someone is using Solamnic signals on the south road and the garrison did not authorise it. This is relevant in Scene 53B.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** The night is dark and clear and empty. Four hours. Nothing to report.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

At the quietest part of the watch — the hour between two and three when even the wind stops — something happens. Not a vision. Not a voice. A feeling, behind your sternum, of being accompanied. Of someone standing watch with you who is not visible and not intrusive and simply: present. It lasts ten minutes. When it ends the cold feels less cold and you are certain, without evidence, that whatever is on the south road will not arrive tonight. A small sign. Small signs count. Mark 1 tally under whichever god feels right.

choices.text: Report the light to Caeron at dawn.
Tell no one. Watch more carefully tomorrow.
Share it with Sera . Two sets of eyes are better.

35.title: THE FORGE
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury Forge, Afternoon*

The forge is the loudest place in Brightholm and the warmest. Bronwyn — the armourer, who has been here longer than Ser Aldous and sees no reason to say so — works the bellows with the steady rhythm of someone whose body stopped distinguishing between work and breathing years ago. The heat is a wall. The sound is a heartbeat. The smell is hot iron, coal, neatsfoot oil, and the specific human smell of someone who has been sweating productively since before dawn.

She does not acknowledge you for three minutes. This is not rudeness — Bronwyn has standards, and one of them is that she finishes what she is doing before she decides whether the interruption is worth her attention. When she does look up, she looks at your sword first and you second. This tells you everything about Bronwyn's hierarchy of values.

'Let me see it.' Not a request. She takes the sword, holds it to the light, turns it twice. Examines the edge where you have been sharpening it yourself. Makes a sound that is not approval but is the absence of disapproval, which from Bronwyn is considerable.

'You sharpen it too far forward,' she says. 'Two inches behind the point is where the work happens in a real fight. Not the tip. The belly.' She shows you. Her hands on the blade are as precise as a surgeon's. In three strokes she changes something you cannot quite identify and when she hands it back the balance is different. Better. You wonder how long the sword has been wrong and how long she has been waiting for you to bring it in.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Investigation (INT, proficiency if trained) (+0) | INT | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** While she works, you notice something on the forge wall — a Solamnic Knight's insignia, old, scorched. Not displayed. Set aside. 'My husband's,' she says, without being asked. 'Knight of the Rose. Died at Vingaard. The insignia survived the fire.' She does not say anything else about it. She does not need to. The forge is hot and the hammer falls and someone who loved a Knight of the Rose is still here, maintaining the swords, and has been for a very long time.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** She finishes her work. You learn about sword maintenance. The forge is loud and hot and that is all.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**ADD TO KNOWLEDGE — SWORD MAINTENANCE**
Bronwyn's correction is permanent. Your longsword now has a +1 bonus to damage rolls against enemies in medium armour or heavier (you know where to aim). This bonus stacks with existing weapon modifiers. Mark this on your character sheet.

choices.text: Ask her about the insignia.
Ask her to teach you more about blade work.
Thank her and leave. She has work to do.
Make camp. The company needs rest.

40.title: THE EASTERN APPROACH
prose: *South Road — Treeline*

You reach the ridge by midmorning. The treeline here is old growth — pine and ash, light coming through in long diagonals. Below: not bandits. A camp. Cooking fires. The smell of something being boiled. Children. There are perhaps thirty people. None of them look like bandits. All of them look frightened.

choices.text: Observe before descending.
Descend and approach openly.

41.title: THE DIRECT APPROACH
prose: *South Road*

The direct road means you are visible from half a mile. You watch them see you. A cluster of adults moves without discussion, putting themselves between the children and your approach. Then one of them sees the tabard — the Solamnic sun — and says something low to the group, and the posture changes. An elderly woman starts crying with the specific relief of someone who has been holding something for too long.

choices.text: Identify yourself and explain the routine exercise.
Ask them where they have come from. Let them talk first.

42.title: THE WEST BANK
prose: *South Road — River Ford*
Three farmsteads on the west bank, recently abandoned. Not weeks — days. Tables still set for a meal that was not finished. A child's wooden horse on the floor near the hearth.

boxes.text: **You Learn:** The Dragon Army has already crossed the river at least once.

choices.text: Take the boot prints evidence to Caeron immediately.
Proceed to the refugee camp with this information.

43.title: THE SPLIT
prose: *South Road*
You come up the ridge from the east and find Aldric already there. He has a Baaz Draconian bound at the wrists.

boxes.text:
**COMBAT ENCOUNTER — THE DETAINED SCOUT**
One wounded Baaz breaks free. You fight alone for 2 rounds before Aldric arrives.
**ENEMIES:** Baaz Draconian: AC 13, HP 14 (wounded), Speed 30 ft.
Enemy Tactics: It tries to disengage and run. If it escapes (not downed in 3 rounds) the patrol arrives early in Scene 44.
Victory: The Baaz falls. Its body hardens to stone in its death throes — you pry your blade free as Aldric arrives at a run. The scout is dead, but now you know the Dragon Army patrols this road.
Rewards: 5 XP.
Fallen (0 HP): Aldric arrives to finish the fight. 1 HP and one exhaustion.

**ADD TO INVENTORY — DRAGON ARMY PATROL SKETCH**
From the detained draconian: a hide map of patrol routes. Show this to Caeron: +1 bonus to the Scene 52 defensive position check.

choices.text: Report the scout to Caeron.
Continue to the refugee camp immediately.

45.title: THESSALY IN THE FIELD
prose: *South Road*
Thessaly moves through the exercise area with practiced near-invisibility. She has her notebook inside her jacket rather than out. She is watching everything and taking no notes yet.

choices.text: Brief her on the refugees.
Ask her to observe only — do not report yet.

63.title: ALDRIC ON THE ROAD
prose: *South Road*

'Do you know what I thought when the draconians hit us on the south road? I thought: good, she is here. Or he. Or they.' He glances over once and then back to the road. 'I was glad it was you. Not someone else. Not anyone else.'

choices.text: 'I was glad it was you too.'
'You fight well.'
Continue riding south in silence (Scene 64)

64.title: RIDING IN SILENCE
prose: *South Road*
A good silence — the kind that only exists between people who do not need to fill it. The smoke on the southern horizon is thicker now.

choices.text: Keep the silence all the way to camp.
After a mile, ask him something you have wanted to ask.
Refugees on the road heading north (Scene 64C)
Take the forest path. Faster, but the wolves are active at night.

65.title: THE ROAD AMBUSH
prose: *South Road — Forest Edge, Day 2*
Arrows from the treeline, then three Dragon Army soldiers and a Sivak Draconian Officer. Not a patrol. Assassins. They have a description of Solamnic squire colours. They have found you.

The Sivak officer barks orders to the soldiers. [LANG:Draconic|*The words are guttural, commanding — you cannot understand them.*]'Sivak-thral vex. The one in Solamnic colours — bring the body to Commander Hestrix. The others, leave for the crows.'[/LANG] The soldiers spread out with practiced efficiency.

boxes.text:
**COMBAT ENCOUNTER — DRAGON ARMY ASSASSIN SQUAD**
**ENEMIES:**
• Human Soldier x3: AC 14, HP 14 each. Longsword +4 1d8+2. Crossbow +4 1d8+2.
• Sivak Draconian (Officer): AC 16, HP 48. Greatsword +6 2d6+4. Speed 40 ft., Fly 60 ft.
• Sivak: Shape Steal — on death by melee, takes the killer's appearance. Commander's Aura: soldiers within 30 ft. get +1 to attacks.
**IMPORTANT:** Do not kill the Sivak in melee — it will Shape Steal your appearance. Use ranged attacks or force it to flee.
Victory: The last soldier falls. The Sivak shrieks, wings beating — it launches into the sky, fleeing into the dark. On the officer's body you find sealed orders. Your name is on them.
Rewards: 50 XP.
Fallen (0 HP): Aldric holds the Sivak while you are stabilised. 1 HP, one exhaustion.

choices.text: The ambush is broken. Continue to Scene 66.

66.title: AFTER THE AMBUSH
prose: *South Road Camp, Night*

The sealed orders have your name in them. Caeron looks at this for a long time. 'They know about the dispatch,' he says. 'And about you.' He folds the paper. 'Good — if they are targeting you specifically, that means what you have been doing has mattered enough.'

boxes.text:
**ROLL REQUIRED:** Investigation (+3) | INT | DC 14
**SUCCESS (roll 14+):** The orders use a standard Blue Wing format except for one line in a different hand containing your specific description. Added by someone with insider access.
**FAILURE (roll 1-13):** The source of that intelligence is unclear.

choices.text: Keep the information close.
Brief the full command.
Find Serath — they may be in danger.

67.title: ALDRIC AT THE RIVER
prose: *Forest River Crossing — Dawn, Day 3*

He tells you about three months before you arrived: he disobeyed an order to turn away refugees at the western gate. He has been waiting to be punished. 'I need to know if you would have done the same.'

boxes.text:
**ROLL REQUIRED:** Persuasion (+0) | CHA | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** +1 Aldric Oath track. ROMANCE OATH activates if Flame was present.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** He nods anyway.

choices.text: 'Yes. I would have done the same.'
'I do not know for certain. But you made the right call.'

68.title: THE LAST CAMP
prose: *Forest Road — Night, Day 3*
The last camp before Vogler. Someone has a flute somewhere down the line — playing every night for twenty years. The melody is old. Tomorrow, Vogler.

boxes.text:
**PALADIN — THE CHOOSING**
All Paladins: after your choice below, turn to Scene P4 now.

**PALADIN — FALLEN TRACK**
If FALLEN on DG track: turn to Scene DG4 now.

**FIGHTER — MEASURE TRACK**
If you have not yet completed M3 and M4: turn to Scene M3 now.

**ROMANCE CONDITIONAL SCENES**
If Sera at FLAME: turn to Scene 68B. If Vael at FLAME: turn to Scene 68C.

choices.text: Tell her the true thing.
'Same thing as before. Not being enough.'
'Right now? Nothing.'
Paladin on DG track: The Reckoning (Scene DG4)
Sera at Flame: the last camp together (Scene 68B)
Vael at Flame: the last camp together (Scene 68C)

69.title: MIRA AT THE CROSSROADS
prose: *Forest Road Junction — Afternoon, Day 3*
The road splits. North to safety. South toward Vogler. Mira stands at the junction. 'I told myself I was going to take the safe road. I am not going to do that.'

choices.text: South. She falls in beside you.
'North. Take the others to safety.'

70.title: THE BRIDGE
prose: *South Road — Vogler Approach, Late Day 3*
The lake appears first. Then the village. And on the bridge into Vogler: a Dragon Army forward element setting up a checkpoint. Four soldiers. They have not seen you yet.

boxes.text:
**ROLL REQUIRED:** Perception (+2) | WIS | DC 14
**SUCCESS (roll 14+):** Two days to Vogler, not three. You warn the group. The party gains surprise.
**FAILURE (roll 1-13):** No surprise — initiative is rolled normally.

**COMBAT ENCOUNTER — THE BRIDGE CHECKPOINT**
**ENEMIES:**
• Human Soldier x4: AC 14, HP 16 each. Longsword +4 1d8+2.
• Sergeant (one of the four): HP 22. Bonus action Shout: one ally rerolls a failed attack.
Victory: The bridge is yours. The sergeant drops his sword and the remaining soldiers scatter. Vogler's gates creak open — the villagers watched the fight from the walls. You enter as the knight who cleared the road.
Rewards: 30 XP.
Fallen (0 HP): Aldric holds the sergeant. You enter Vogler at 1 HP. Villagers treat your wounds.

choices.text: Proceed to Scene 71.

71.title: THE FIRST SIGHT OF VOGLER
prose: *Vogler — Evening*

Vogler in the late afternoon has the quality of a place that has been bracing itself and is briefly, exhaustedly, relieved. The people watching from the walls as you crossed the bridge have the faces of people for whom Solamnic armour is the best thing they have seen this week.

Behind you, the Dragon Army is moving. Behind you, also, are weeks that changed the shape of what you are. You are a squire now — sworn, blooded, tired in the specific way of someone who has been tested at intervals and kept passing.
Whatever comes next is the main campaign. This was how it began.
**Motto:** Est Sularus oth Mithas — My honour is my life.

You earned this not through outstanding sword work but through asking the right questions and standing by the answers. The Rose values wisdom and moral courage. Ser Aldous was present at your oath. He did not try to hide that he was moved.

**Your Road to Vogler:** You ride into Vogler carrying the weight of what justice means when law fails it. Start with +2 Persuasion and the trait: Voice of the Rose (NPCs who see you protect someone at personal cost trust you immediately).
**Motto:** The blade that does not waver.
You earned this on the south road, on the ridge, at the bridge — in every fight where you chose to stand rather than step back. Aldric was in the front row at your oath. You saw him.
**Your Road to Vogler:** You ride into Vogler knowing what is coming will need everything you have. Start with +2 Athletics and the trait: Blade-Oath (advantage on first attack of any combat).
**Motto:** Law is the shield of the innocent.

You kept the law when others would have broken it and understood that duty is the structure that lets conscience act in the world. Lord Caeron conducted your squiring ceremony himself. Afterwards, in private, he shook your hand instead of saluting. That is not in the protocol.

**Your Road to Vogler:** You ride into Vogler as the Order's standard. People will look to you when things fall apart. Start with +2 Persuasion/Intimidation and the trait: Command Presence (NPCs do not panic in your presence during combat).

boxes.text:
**Your Prelude Ends Here**
Turn to the Order Outcome section for your squiring ceremony and title. Then turn to the Romance Epilogue section. Then turn to THE BRIDGE TO THE CAMPAIGN.

**Squire Title Granted**

72.title: ALDRIC VOSS
prose: He finds you after the squiring when everyone else has gone. 'I spent eight months being competitive with you. I think that was because I did not know what else to do with it.' He looks at you. 'I know now.' He says I love you with staying — with showing up — with the way his hand finds yours. In the main campaign, Aldric Voss is a named companion with his own quest line.

73.title: THESSALY BRIGHTWATER
prose: She interviews you one last time. Except this time: 'What do you want people to remember about what happened here?' She writes it down carefully. Then she closes the notebook. Sets it on the wall. Looks at you without the professional distance. 'I stopped being a journalist about you a while ago.' She puts it down. In the main campaign, Thessaly provides intelligence and covers you.

choices.text: Next epilogue.

74.title: COMMANDER SERATH
prose: They find you just before dawn. 'I am trying to become the kind of person who deserves it. I have not succeeded yet. But I am trying. I have not been trying for anyone before.' In the main campaign, Serath is a morally complex ally.

75.title: MIRA OF THE OAKS
prose: She walks beside you on the last road. She does not touch you, and she does not need to. At some point she says, very quietly: 'I was not looking for this.' You were not either. In the main campaign, Mira is the party's healer and the person you fight hardest to keep standing.

76.title: LORD CAERON ASHVALE
prose: He gives you your evaluation report. In the Overall Assessment box he has written: 'This squire will be remarkable. I have no further notes.' He stops at the door. 'The Order of the Crown values constancy. I intend to be constant.' In the main campaign, Caeron provides strategic resources and a quest line about what he is hiding.

77.title: SERA NIGHTHOLLOW
prose: She is already awake and has scouted the perimeter. She says: 'I should tell you that I am not easy to be with. I have been told this.' You tell her you are not particularly easy either. Then, not smiling but with the thing behind her eyes that is equivalent: 'All right.' That is everything. In the main campaign, Sera Nighthollow is a scout companion with her own quest line.

78.title: VAEL ASHMORE
prose: She does not do the thing where people are strange in the morning after something real. She is exactly herself — a little softer. She hands you your gear and says: 'For what it is worth, I have no regrets.' Then you are both laughing. 'I want to do this again. After. When there is an after.' In the main campaign, Vael Ashmore is a Crown-path companion with her own quest line.

79.title: TARIEL DUSKMANTLE
prose: She does not take the Oath. She watches you take it from the back of the chapel with her notebook open and her pen still. She writes one line. Afterward: 'I have decided to stay. Not as an observer.' She takes the credential and folds it and puts it in your hand. 'I do not need this anymore. I know what I am observing.' In the main campaign, Tariel Duskmantle is a political advisor with no rank, no oath, and the most accurate assessment of power in any room she enters. She loves you the way she loves an argument: completely, with her full attention, and with the knowledge that the conclusion may change.

80.title: LYSANDER GRAVES
prose: He is not at the ceremony. He is at the gate, already mounted, facing south. On the road he does not ride beside you — he rides near. The night before Vogler: 'I will be at the wall. Whatever they need me to be. Sword for hire. No title, no Oath, no complications.' He looks at you. 'Except one.' In the main campaign, Lysander Graves is an unaffiliated fighter with no rank and the best sword arm in Vogler. He is the most dangerous kind of complication: a good man without a system, in love with someone inside one.
