3B.title: THE ARMOURY ROOF
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Training Yard, Dawn*

She is sitting on the armoury roof with her legs hanging over the edge and a whetstone working a long knife with the slow patience of someone who has been awake for a while. She watches you spar below — or watches Aldric, specifically, with the professional attention of someone cataloguing how he moves.

After a while she calls down without raising her voice: 'Your footwork has a gap on the left. He overcommits on the third combination. If you wait him out, he gives it to you.' Then she goes back to the whetstone. You have not been introduced. You do not know her name.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION JOINS: SERA NIGHTHOLLOW**
Garrison scout-rogue. She sees things. She tells you when she feels like it. Possible love interest — the slow kind.
Role: Scout intelligence: she knows the roads, the patrol patterns, and who is lying. If you listen to her, she keeps talking.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+2) | WIS | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** She has been here for at least an hour — the whetstone marks on the roof edge show she worked on that knife before the sparring started. She was not watching Aldric. She was watching the approach to the courtyard. She is a scout, and she came here to observe something. Possibly you.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** She is sitting on the roof sharpening a knife. That is all you can read.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Call up: 'I know. I have been trying to get him to show it for three weeks.' She looks at you with new attention.
Ignore the advice. You do not know her.
Ask her name. She considers. Then tells you.

5B.title: THE SAME DENT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Assessment Hall Corridor*

You are both waiting outside the assessment hall for your individual results. The corridor is narrow. She is looking at the wall. You are looking at your sword. After a while she glances over, sees the pommel dent, and without any preamble holds up her own sword. The same dent. Same location. Same cause, probably.

'Caeron?' she says. You nod. 'He gave me satisfactory too.' A pause. 'I think satisfactory is actually his highest mark.' She says this with the slight bitterness of someone who has thought about it a lot and decided to find it funny instead of devastating.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION JOINS: VAEL ASHMORE**
Knight-squire candidate, your equal in training. She has been here six months. She noticed you three weeks ago and did not say anything until now.
Role: Mutual support: once per Act, you may ask Vael's read on a situation. She gives honest assessments, even when they are not what you want to hear.

**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+1) | WIS | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** She has been here six months and knows exactly how long it takes Caeron to review each squire's assessment. She timed her appearance in this corridor. She has been looking for a reason to speak to you for longer than this morning. ROMANCE SPARK Vael at Advantage if you acknowledge this.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** She is waiting for her results. The timing seems coincidental.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: 'Satisfactory is the ceiling. I decided that this morning.' She looks at you. Recalibrates.
'I think you are right. Which is worse somehow.' She laughs.
'We should compare notes on how to fix it.' Practical. She approves.

11B.title: A FAMILIAR FACE IN THE COURTYARD
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Main Courtyard*

At the edge of the morning formation there is a young man you have not seen before. Not a garrison squire — his armour is older, a knight's kit that has been carefully maintained through years of use rather than stored and polished for ceremony. The Brightblade family crest. He stands in the formation anyway, because standing in formation is what you do, and he is clearly the kind of person who does what you do.

Ser Aldous speaks to him after formation. A brief exchange. The young man listens with the complete attention of someone who does not take instruction for granted. He nods once. He does not look as though things are going particularly well for him. He looks as though he intends to do the honourable thing regardless.

When formation breaks he catches your eye. A slight nod — not casual, formal. The greeting of one squire to another. His name is Sturm. He is passing through on his way south and needed a place to sleep. 'The Knighthood still stands,' he says, when you ask why he wears the armour when he could wear anything. 'Someone should act as though it does.'

The spar with Aldric is part of the morning routine. After the spar — and after formation, where Lord Caeron reads out the day's assignments — the real decisions begin. The field exercise on the south road is tomorrow. Today is preparation. How you spend it matters.

boxes.text:
**DRAGONLANCE CAMEO — OPTIONAL SCENE**
This scene is non-mandatory flavour. It introduces a canon character from the Dragonlance world who may reappear in the main campaign.

**Who Is Sturm Brightblade?**
Sturm Brightblade is one of the Companions of the Lance — the heroes of the War of the Lance. At this point in the timeline he is a squire himself, working to prove himself worthy of the Knighthood his father abandoned. He takes the Oath of Measures more seriously than most knights. He will fight for anyone who cannot fight for themselves. He will die for it one day. Right now he is just a young man who sleeps in garrison stables when he has to and acts like a knight when he does not have to.

choices.text: Tell him you admire the conviction. He looks surprised — not many say that.
Ask him about his father. He is quiet a moment. 'He left the Order. I chose not to.'
Nod back. Let the moment be what it is.
Plan the exercise with Aldric together.
Find Thessaly first — she may have intelligence.
Train harder. You want to be sharp.
Check on Mira before you march.
Check the infirmary. There was mention of refugees arriving in the night.
Go to the mess hall. Eat. Watch. Learn.
Take a night watch shift. The wall has things to teach you.
Visit the forge. Your blade needs attention.

35A.title: THE INSIGNIA
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury Forge, Afternoon*
You ask about the insignia on the wall. Bronwyn does not stop working. The hammer falls twice before she speaks.

‘Knight of the Rose. Ser Davan Ironhand.’ The name comes out like something she has said ten thousand times and will say ten thousand more. ‘He believed the Oath was not a set of words. He believed it was a thing you did with your hands, every day, until the day you could not.’
She turns the metal in the tongs. The glow lights her face from below.

‘He died holding a bridge at Vingaard. Not because he was ordered to. Because there were people behind him who could not hold it themselves. That is what the Rose means. Not wisdom in the abstract. Wisdom applied — knowing what matters and standing in front of it.’
She sets the hammer down. Looks at you directly for the first time.
‘You sharpen your sword. That is good. But a sword is a tool. The question is what you point it at and why. Get that right and the edge will take care of itself.’
She picks up the hammer again. The conversation is over. But something has shifted in how she looks at you — not warmth, exactly, but recognition. You are someone who asked the right question.

boxes.text: **Bronwyn’s Trust:** Bronwyn will remember this. Future visits to the forge may offer additional options.

choices.text: Return to the afternoon.

35B.title: BLADE WORK
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury Forge, Afternoon*
Bronwyn looks at you for a long moment. Then she nods — once, the way she does everything, with no wasted motion.
‘Stand there. Sword out. No — not like that. Like you mean to use it, not wave it about.’

For the next hour she takes apart everything you think you know about fighting. Not your technique — your understanding. She shows you where armour is weakest, where joints flex, where a blade finds the gap between plate and chain. She makes you strike a hanging sack of sand until your arms burn, then corrects your grip and makes you do it again.

‘You are strong enough,’ she says, which is not a compliment but an observation. ‘Strength is common. Precision is not. Any fool can hit hard. Hitting the right place at the right time with the right angle — that is craft. That is what keeps you alive when the fool beside you goes down.’
By the end your sword arm aches and your edge awareness has changed permanently. You see the lines of a fight differently now — not just where to swing but where a swing will do the most work.
She takes your sword again, runs a whetstone along the edge with three precise strokes, and hands it back. ‘Come back when you have dulled it. Not before.’ High praise, from Bronwyn.

boxes.text:
**ADD TO KNOWLEDGE — BRONWYN’S BLADE TRAINING**
Bronwyn’s instruction is permanent. You gain +1 to damage rolls with your primary melee weapon against armoured targets. This stacks with existing bonuses.

choices.text: Return to the afternoon.

35C.title: RESPECT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — Armoury Forge, Afternoon*

You thank her and turn to leave. Bronwyn makes a sound — not quite acknowledgement, not quite dismissal. Something between the two that means she noticed you did not waste her time and she respects that.

At the door you glance back. She is already working again, the hammer falling in its steady rhythm, the forge breathing its orange heat into the afternoon air. The insignia on the wall catches the light — a Knight of the Rose who died at Vingaard, maintained by the woman who loved him, in a forge that keeps the living armed.
Some people show their loyalty with words. Bronwyn shows hers with steel and fire and thirty years of not leaving.

choices.text: Return to the afternoon.

M1.title: THE LETTER AND THE SPIRIT
prose: *Castle Brightholm — During Act One (Fighter Only)*

Ser Aldous stops you in the corridor two days after your conversation in the library. He has a book under his arm — not the Measure, something older, the binding cracked. 'Walk with me,' he says. He takes you to the garrison's east wall and shows you the southern road. A traveller is being turned away at the gate. The traveller has valid papers. The gate guard has a standing order: no travellers past the third bell. It is not yet the third bell by four minutes. The gate guard is being technically correct and functionally unkind.

boxes.text:
**FIGHTER ONLY**
This scene becomes available after Scene 8 (Ser Aldous on Justice). Read Scene 8 first. Paladins: your divine scenes (P-track) cover this ground differently — skip to Scene 9.

**THE MEASURE: THE MEASURE ON INTERPRETATION**
The Measure was written to govern knights, not gate guards. But the same logic applies: the law is only as good as its interpretation. A law followed with malice is not the Measure. A law followed with judgment is. The question Aldous is asking, without asking it: when you hold the Measure, will you hold the letter or the intention?

choices.text: Speak to the gate guard. The traveller has valid papers and the bell has not rung. The order should be followed as written — which means the traveller passes.
Let it go. The guard is following orders. The traveller will find lodging. This is not a Measure problem.
Ask the guard why. Not to correct him — to understand his reasoning. Then, depending on the answer, decide.

M1_letter.title: THE LETTER WELL USED
prose: *Castle Brightholm East Gate*

The guard is annoyed but cannot argue. The traveller passes. Aldous watches you the whole time with the expression of someone marking a test paper he expected to be harder. 'The letter well used,' he says. 'You can defend that decision at any Measure tribunal in Solamnia. You can also defend it to the traveller. Both matter.' He hands you the old book. 'Read the third chapter when you have time. It is about what happens when the letter is used badly.'

boxes.text:
**MEASURE TRACK: +2 — THE LETTER**
You are learning the Measure as a tool. Mark +2 on your Measure track. How you use this learning in M3 and M4 will shape your gift.

choices.text: Continue to Scene 9.

M1_restrain.title: THE RESTRAINT
prose: *Castle Brightholm East Gate*

You let it go. The guard is following his orders. The traveller leaves. Aldous nods slowly. 'Restraint is its own form of judgment,' he says. 'The Measure does not require you to intervene in everything. Knowing when not to act is as important as knowing when to act.' He pauses. 'The traveller will be fine. The guard will keep doing this. At some point you will have to decide whether that concerns you.'

boxes.text:
**MEASURE TRACK: +1 — RESTRAINT**
Mark +1. Your path leans toward deliberate judgment over immediate action. This will matter at M3.

choices.text: Continue to Scene 9.

M1_ask.title: THE QUESTION
prose: *Castle Brightholm East Gate*

The guard's reason: he was told the order means no exceptions and he does not want trouble. You let the traveller through. Then you tell the guard, simply: 'The order says third bell. Four minutes before the bell is before the bell.' You do not report him. You do not lecture him. Aldous says nothing for a while. Then: 'You asked why before you judged. Most people do it the other way around. Most of the time that is a mistake.'

boxes.text:
**MEASURE TRACK: +3 — THE QUESTION**
Highest first-scene gain. Mark +3. You are learning the Measure through understanding, not just rule. This path is the hardest and the most useful.

choices.text: Continue to Scene 9.

62D.title: THE FIRST NIGHT ON THE ROAD
prose: *South Road — Day 1, Evening Camp*

The first night out of Brightholm is the hardest. Not because of danger — the road is clear for eight miles — but because of what leaving means. The garrison towers were visible until the fifth mile, then the ridge took them. Brightholm is behind you.

Camp is set with garrison efficiency. The fire is regulation size, which means small and purposeful. You eat garrison rations: hard bread, dried meat, a cheese that has survived the march better than expected. Not good food. Adequate food, eaten in the company of people you have spent the most important weeks of your life with, which makes it the best food you have ever eaten.
**IF TARIEL IS IN YOUR PARTY:** She eats apart from the column. She has her notebook open. Once, she looks up and catches your eye. The moment lasts three seconds. She looks down first.
**IF LYSANDER IS IN YOUR PARTY:** He is not in the camp. He rides parallel, not with, the column. Visible occasionally at the treeline, then not visible.

boxes.text:
**NPC CROSS-TALK — CAERON AND SERA**
At the edge of camp: Lord Caeron is speaking with Sera Nighthollow. Caeron's careful listening posture; Sera's clipped precision. When they finish, Caeron nods once. Sera glances at you briefly. Whatever she told him, it was about the road ahead.

choices.text: Walk the perimeter before sleeping.
Find your companions. Sit with them.
Watch the stars. Solinari is rising.
Train together as a group in the morning.
Share a quiet meal together.

64B.title: THE WAR SIGNS
prose: *South Road — Day 2, Morning*

The road changes at the tenth mile. The farms are empty — not abandoned, emptied deliberately. At the eleventh mile: a cart on its side, a pair of child's shoes tied together by the laces. At the twelfth mile: a Solamnic mile-marker with fresh chalk. Someone wrote SOUTH NO. Below it, in a different hand: ARMY 2 DAYS.
The column does not comment. The silence deepens.

choices.text: Stop. Pick up the child's shoes. Carry them.
Note the chalk for your report.
Keep moving.
Watch the moons with whoever will join you.

64C.title: REFUGEES GOING NORTH
prose: *South Road — Day 2, Afternoon*
Eleven people moving north. You are moving south. The man with the child stops when he sees the tabard. He says: 'Vogler. Please.'
**IF YOU SAVED THE REFUGEES IN ACT TWO:** One of the eleven recognises you. 'You. You saved us. My daughter is alive because of you.'
**IF YOU TURNED THE REFUGEES BACK:** You do not know what happened to those thirty people. The not-knowing sits in you like a stone.

choices.text: Give them your rations.
Brief them on the road north.
Keep moving.

66B.title: CAMP CONVERSATIONS
prose: *Forest Road — Day 2, Night Camp*
The fire is smaller tonight — Caeron's order. The conversations become the kind that happen in the dark between people aware that remaining conversations may be finite.
**Choose one companion to spend the evening with:**

66B_Aldric.title: WHAT HE CARRIES
prose: He tells you about his mother. She died when he was twelve. 'I compete with you because I do not know another way to tell someone they matter to me. I am working on it. It is slow.'

choices.text: 'I know. I have always known.'
'You are better at it than you think.'

66B_Thessaly.title: WHAT SHE FEARS
prose: 'I am afraid of writing the history that says they fell.' She opens the locket. Inside: a page from a letter her mother wrote the week before the Cataclysm. 'When the world changes, the people who write it down are the ones who decide what it meant.'

choices.text: 'You are. I have seen you work.'
'Then write the version where we hold.'

66B_Mira.title: WHAT SHE KNOWS
prose: 'I know what two thousand soldiers means. In medical terms. I know the arithmetic does not work.' She is not afraid. She is past afraid. 'I will be there anyway.'

choices.text: 'The arithmetic never accounts for the people who refuse to stop.'
Help her count. The counting helps.

66B_Serath.title: WHAT THEY LOST
prose: 'I had a unit. Twelve people. When I left, I left them inside. They are in the army marching on Vogler. I may see people I know across the line. I will not hesitate. But I want you to know it costs something.'

choices.text: 'I will stand beside you when it does.'
'If you need to step back, I will cover you.'

66B_Caeron.title: WHAT HE HIDES
prose: 'I have never led people into a situation where I cannot guarantee their survival.' He turns. His face is the face of a man who is thirty years old and does not know if he is enough. 'I wanted someone to know.'

choices.text: 'You are enough. We will hold.'
'Then we hold for as long as we can.'

66B_Tariel.title: THE QUESTION
prose: 'If the Oath is what you say it is — why is Vogler undefended? Why were the refugees turned south? Why is a squire being sent where a regiment should be?'
**CONVICTION CHOICE (FALLEN is possible here):**
**ROMANCE CHOICE (optional — if at FLAME or higher):**

choices.text: 'Because the institution is failing does not mean the principle is wrong. I am the principle.'
'I do not have an answer. I have the Oath and Vogler and the people beside me.'
'You are right. The institution is insufficient. I am taking the Oath anyway because the people at Vogler need someone who shows up.'
'The Measure is a beautiful relic. I will carry it because someone should, but I no longer believe it is enough.'
'I believe in the Order. I have heard everything you said and I choose this anyway.'
'Stay. Not for the Order. For me.'
'Thank you. For all of it.'
Do not address.

66B_Lysander.title: THE LAST FIRE
prose: 'I took the Oath at nineteen. I meant every word. I stopped meaning them when the system rewarded the decision to let people die and punished the decision to save them, and did both correctly according to its own rules.'
'I am not telling you not to take the Oath. I am telling you: the words are not the thing. You are the thing.'
**CONVICTION CHOICE (FALLEN is possible here):**
**ROMANCE CHOICE (optional — if at FLAME or higher):**

choices.text: 'I will take the Oath. And I will mean it the way you meant it — the real version.'
'The words are not the thing. The Oath is a formality I will perform because Vogler needs it, not because I believe the institution will save anyone.'
'The Oath means what I decide it means. What I decide, in the moment, with the people in front of me.'
'I hear you. I respect you. And I am going to take the Oath and mean every word because the alternative is a world where no one tries.'
'I do not know what the Oath means yet. I will figure it out after.'
'Stay with me tonight.'
Take his hand. Let the fire burn down.
'Thank you.'
Do not address.

68B.title: THE LAST CAMP — SERA
prose: *Forest Road — Night, Day 3 (if Sera Oath-track active)*

The camp is quiet. The watch has changed. Sera is not on rotation tonight — she has not slept in forty hours and you told her you would take the first watch if she told you where she was going. She told you.

You find her at the far edge of the firelight where it thins into darkness, sitting with her back against a tree and her knife across her knees not because she thinks she needs it but because having it there is how she slows down. She looks up at you. The half-dark makes her face different — softer, less defended, the way people look when they think no one is watching.

boxes.text:
**CONDITIONAL SCENE**
This scene occurs if Sera is at Flame level or above.

**ROMANCE MOMENT**
She says: 'You came.' Like she did not expect it. Like she expected it. Like both are true. You sit down against the same tree and the distance between you is not much. She tilts her head and studies your face with the focused attention she normally gives to terrain. Then she says: 'I have been thinking about this since the south road.' She does not elaborate. She does not need to.

choices.text: 'So have I.' Two words. She holds your gaze for a long moment. Then she puts the knife down and turns toward you and the rest of the watch is not spent watching.
'Tell me what you found in that village. The real version.' She tells you. It is terrible. You hold it together. Sometime before dawn the holding becomes something else.
'I did not want to get to Vogler without saying something.' She considers this. Then: 'What are you saying?' You tell her.

68C.title: THE LAST CAMP — VAEL
prose: *Forest Road — Night, Day 3 (if Vael Oath-track active)*

She is cleaning her equipment for the third time, which is how you know she is not actually thinking about her equipment. She looks up when you approach with the expression of someone who has made a decision and is not sure they have the nerve to act on it.

She says: 'I want to say something before tomorrow and I need you to let me finish it because I am going to say it badly.' She looks at her sword. 'I have been here six months and the only person whose opinion of me I actually care about is yours. I did not plan for that. I do not know what to do with it. But we are one day from Vogler and I am not going to be someone who did not say it.'

boxes.text:
**CONDITIONAL SCENE**
This scene occurs if Vael is at Flame level or above.

**ROMANCE MOMENT**
She is looking at you now. The firelight is behind you and her expression is completely unguarded — all the competitive competence and dry deflection set aside, just: a person who told you a true thing and is waiting to see what you do with it. She is slightly breathless. She would hate knowing that you can see that.

choices.text: 'I know. I have known for a while. I did not know what to do with it either.' She exhales. Somewhere in that exhale is a laugh. Then she closes the distance and so do you.
'You said it exactly right.' You mean this. She blinks. Then she sets down the sword and reaches for you.
Say nothing. Close the distance instead. She makes a small surprised sound and then she is kissing you back like she has been waiting to for weeks, which she has.
(Paladin) Speak to Paladine. ‘I am yours, if you will have me. I will guard what is good.’
(Paladin) Speak to Kiri-Jolith. ‘I will fight. I will not waste what you give me.’
(Paladin) Speak to Mishakal. ‘I will heal. I will not choose who deserves it.’
(Paladin) Speak to Habbakuk. ‘I will protect what cannot protect itself.’
(Paladin) Speak to Branchala. ‘I will hold onto the possibility of beauty.’
(Paladin) Remain silent. ‘I am not ready to name you.’

DG4.title: THE RECKONING
prose: *Vogler Road — The Day Before Vogler (Paladin Who Has Fallen)*

You are on the last road before Vogler. The column has stopped for water. You are sitting on a stone wall looking at the lake in the distance when you hear footsteps approaching — not Aldric's, not Mira's. The figure is old. Very old. Robes that are neither white nor grey but the specific colour of very old wood. A face like a map of decisions made and paid for. He sits down without being invited and looks at the lake with you for a while.

'You've been carrying something that isn't yours,' he says. Not unkindly. Factually. 'It is not permanent. But it does not come off easily. The question is whether you want it off badly enough.' He looks at you. 'Not whether you are sorry. Whether you want to be different.' He waits.

This is the hardest question the prelude asks. Not 'did you fall' — you know the answer to that. But whether the falling was truly the end of something or whether it was a lesson you are willing to carry forward. The answer has to be real. He will know if it isn't.

boxes.text:
**FALLEN PALADINS ONLY**
If you are HELD, HELD FIRM, PURIFIED, or PULLED BACK: skip to Scene 70.

**DRAGONLANCE: ELISTAN OF PALANTHAS (or a messenger in his name)**
Elistan was the first Chosen of Paladine after the Cataclysm — the first person in three hundred years to carry true divine power. He did not come to judge you. He came because someone asked him to find you.

choices.text: Yes. I want to be different. I want to understand what I chose and unmake it.
I want to be different but I don't know if I can. That's the honest answer.
I want redemption more than anything. But I don't have the path.
I made a rational choice. I stand by it.

DG4_recover.title: THE RETURN
prose: *Vogler Road — Recovery*

He nods once. He puts his hand on your head for a moment — briefly, the way a blessing works when it is genuinely meant — and then he stands and walks away. The road is the same road. The lake is the same lake. Your hands feel clean. Not new-clean. Clean the way things are clean when they have been used and washed and used again. Your god's warmth is back in full, unfiltered, entirely present.

boxes.text:
**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE VOW RESTORED**
*Wondrous Item (Requires Attunement by a Paladin), Uncommon*
A small medallion of white-silver, shaped like a closed fist holding a flame.
**Mechanics:** Once per long rest: when you use Lay on Hands, you may also remove one condition: frightened, charmed, or poisoned. Advantage on saving throws against being charmed or possessed by fiends or undead.
**Lore:** Carried by one who fell and chose to return.

choices.text: Proceed to Scene 70.

DG4_partial.title: THE SCAR
prose: *Vogler Road — Partial Recovery*

He does not argue with the honesty. 'That is a better answer than certainty,' he says. Your Lay on Hands is restored. The muffled quality in your prayers is mostly gone. But there is still a small thing — a faint violet note to the golden warmth.

boxes.text:
**MARK ON YOUR CHARACTER SHEET — FAULTY DIVINE POWERS**
Lay on Hands: restored to full pool. Spell slots: each time you expend a spell slot, roll 1d6 first. On a 1 or 2, the slot is spent but the spell fails. This instability ends if you complete the Quest of the Returning Flame.

**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE SHARD OF RETURNING LIGHT**
*Wondrous Item (Requires Attunement by a Paladin), Uncommon — GROWING*
A sliver of white crystal that throws no reflection and casts no shadow. It is slightly warm.
**Mechanics:** Advantage on saves against being frightened. Once per long rest, as a bonus action: creatures of your choice within 10 ft. that are frightened or charmed may reroll their saving throw with advantage. QUEST STATE: grows stronger when quest is completed.
**Lore:** Given to a paladin whose faith is cracked but not broken. The crystal holds light it hasn't earned yet, on credit.

**QUEST: THE RETURNING FLAME**
Complete in Shadow of the Dragon Queen: Find the Shrine of Mishakal (or any good-aligned god shrine). Spend a full night in honest prayer. In the same session, perform one act of genuine self-sacrifice for a stranger. When both conditions are met: faulty spell mechanic ends permanently. The Shard upgrades: bonus action also grants 2d6 temp HP. Once per long rest: cast Protection from Evil and Good without a slot.

choices.text: Proceed to Scene 70.

DG4_ember.title: THE EMBER
prose: *Vogler Road — The Wanting Without the Way*

Elistan is quiet for a long time. 'That is the hardest state,' he says at last. 'Wanting and not having the path. It is not failure. It is the condition that precedes everything real.' He gives you something small and closes your fingers around it.
Your Lay on Hands pool is restored to half normal (rounded up). Your divine abilities still function — unevenly, with effort. But the wanting is a kind of fire, and fire is not nothing.

boxes.text:
**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE EMBER THAT REMAINED**
*Wondrous Item (Requires Attunement by a Paladin), Uncommon — SEEKING*
A small piece of charcoal that is faintly warm at the centre when you hold it in a closed fist.
**Mechanics:** Lay on Hands pool is half normal maximum (rounded up). Once per long rest: spend 10 minutes in honest prayer to cast Guidance on yourself without a slot. Each time you act in direct service of an innocent without personal benefit, mark one tally. At 5 tallies, the ember becomes The Vow Restored. Tallies reset if you knowingly act against innocents.
**Lore:** Given to a paladin who wanted the way back and didn't have it yet. The gods of good do not require certainty — they require the wanting.

choices.text: Proceed to Scene 70.

DG4_fallen.title: THE GIFT OF THE DARK QUEEN
prose: *Vogler Road — No Recovery*

He looks at you for a long moment. 'You believe it,' he says. 'That is real.' He stands. He does not touch you. 'When you understand what you chose,' he says, 'the door is still open. It is always open. The gods of good do not lock doors.' He walks away. That night, something else leaves gifts at the edge of your bedroll. They are beautiful. They are cold.

boxes.text:
**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE QUEEN'S BRAND**
*Wondrous Item (Requires Attunement), Uncommon — TAINTED*
A black iron ring inlaid with five colours of enamel — red, blue, black, green, white — in the pattern of five dragon heads.
**Mechanics:** Lay on Hands heals normally. Once per long rest: add 1d6 necrotic damage to a melee attack. However: at dawn each day, DC 12 WIS save or take 1 point of Exhaustion. Removing the ring requires Remove Curse or Greater Restoration.
**Lore:** A gift from Takhisis to a paladin she considers an investment. The Queen of Darkness plays a long game.

**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE UNASKED QUESTION**
*Weapon (Longsword), Requires Attunement, Rare — TAINTED*
A longsword with a blade of black steel that reflects nothing. The crossguard is five interlocking crescents.
**Mechanics:** +1 to attack and damage rolls. Once per long rest: on a hit, target makes DC 13 WIS save or is frightened until end of their next turn. Whenever you use this ability, make a DC 12 WIS save yourself or take 1 Exhaustion. The sword cannot be lost — if separated, it reappears in your pack within 24 hours.
**Lore:** A gift from Takhisis to a paladin she considers a long-term investment. It can be unmade only at the Shrine of Paladine by someone who genuinely no longer wants what it offers.

**✦ MAGIC ITEM GAINED: THE SHADOW MANTLE**
*Wondrous Item (Cloak), Requires Attunement, Rare — TAINTED*
A cloak of deep violet-black that absorbs the light around you slightly.
**Mechanics:** +1 to AC. Advantage on Stealth checks in dim light or darkness. Once per long rest: become heavily obscured to creatures more than 5 ft. away until start of your next turn. Disadvantage on Persuasion checks with clerics and paladins of good-aligned gods. Removing the cloak requires a DC 14 WIS save at dawn. On failure, you feel no desire to remove it.
**Lore:** Takhisis gives good gifts. That is the point.

**YOUR PALADIN ABILITIES — THE SECRET PATRON**
Until you formally pledge to Takhisis in Shadow of the Dragon Queen, your paladin abilities continue to function — but their source has changed. Lay on Hands heals normally. Divine Smite is very slightly violet. Spell slots function. You are, right now, a paladin of Takhisis who does not know it yet.

**QUEST: THE PLEDGE OF THE FIVE HEADS**
In Shadow of the Dragon Queen: Takhisis will reach for you at moments of violence, desperation, or victory. When ready, seek a Dragon Army officer of Black Wing Command who carries her symbol. The pledge is private, brief, and irreversible. Upon pledging: Oathbreaker subclass. Channel Divinity becomes Animate Dead and Aura of Hate. Lay on Hands can harm enemies. The redemption arc remains available. Elistan said the door is always open.

choices.text: Proceed to Scene 70.

69B.title: THE LAKE AT DAWN
prose: *South Road — Day 3, Final Approach*

The lake appears first as light — the reflection of the sunrise off water, a brightness on the southern horizon that is different from sky-brightness, flatter and wider and the colour of beaten silver. Then the water. Then the village.

Vogler in the early morning is beautiful. That is the thing no one warned you about — that the place you are riding to defend is genuinely, quietly, achingly beautiful. Stone buildings along the waterfront. Boats at the pier. Smoke from morning cooking fires. A stand of aspens at the east end that have gone gold with autumn and are catching the sunrise in a way that looks like the trees are on fire in the good way.
Aldric rides beside you. He sees the lake and for a moment his face does something it almost never does: it opens. Then it reassembles. But you saw it.
**IF MIRA IS WITH YOU:** She stops her horse. 'My mother's village looked like this.' Very quiet. Then she rides on.
**IF TARIEL IS WITH YOU:** Her notebook stays closed. This is not an observation she is willing to reduce to writing.

**IF LYSANDER IS WITH YOU:** He is standing at the ridgeline, separate from the column, looking at the lake. He looks exactly like a Knight of the Sword on the morning of a battle, which is what he is whether the paperwork agrees or not.

choices.text: Stop. Look. Remember this.
'It is worth saving.'
Ride on. The beauty does not change the numbers.
The morning with both of them.

BOND_CAMP.title: AROUND THE FIRE
prose: *Camp — Evening*

The fire is the right size tonight — not the regulation minimum that Caeron insists on, but the slightly larger version that happens when someone adds a log without being asked and no one corrects them. The column has settled into the particular rhythm of people who have been moving together long enough to develop unspoken protocols: who fills the water, who checks the perimeter, who tells the story.

Aldric is cleaning his blade. He always cleans his blade. It is the thing he does when he wants to be present without having to speak, and it works — the repetitive motion gives his hands somewhere to be while the rest of him listens.

Tonight someone asks a question. It is the kind of question that only gets asked around fires, in the dark, when the day is far enough behind and tomorrow is far enough ahead that the present moment feels briefly safe.
'What will you do when this is over?'

boxes.text:
**PARTY BONDING SCENE**
This scene deepens your connection with your companions. All companions present will react.

choices.text: 'Go home. See my family. Then come back.'
'Keep going. There is more road.'
'I do not know yet. Ask me at Vogler.'
Ask someone else the question instead.

BOND_TRAIN.title: TRAINING TOGETHER
prose: *Camp — Morning*

Dawn training has become a group ritual. Not ordered — evolved. Aldric runs sword forms. Vael, if present, matches him pass for pass, the two of them moving through the same combinations with the synchronised precision of people who have been watching each other improve. Sera, if present, watches from the armoury roof or a convenient tree with the professional attention of someone filing observations for later use.
Today someone suggests a change: train together. Not the formal pairs Caeron assigns. Real training — the kind where you learn from each other's differences.

boxes.text:
**OPTIONAL ROLL:** Athletics (+3) | STR | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** You learn a technique from the session. +1 to your next combat encounter attack roll.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** Good exercise. No specific breakthrough.

choices.text: Spar with everyone willing. Learn their styles.
Focus on teaching. Show them what you know.
Ask Sera or Serath for tactical advice instead.
Run the perimeter with whoever will join you.

BOND_MEAL.title: BREAKING BREAD
prose: *Camp — Midday*

Mira, if present, has done something to the garrison rations that borders on alchemy. The bread is the same bread. The cheese is the same cheese. But somehow the combination, with whatever herbs she found at the roadside this morning, has become something worth sitting down for.

If Mira is not present, Aldric has attempted to cook. The result is edible in the way that garrison food is always edible: it sustains life without celebrating it. He is aware of this. He is not apologising.

People sit closer than they used to. That is the change you notice — not the food, not the conversation, but the physical distance. Eight months ago these were strangers. Now they are the people whose breathing you recognise in the dark.

boxes.text:
**PARTY BONDING SCENE**
Share a meal. Listen. Be present.

choices.text: Tell a story from home. Something true.
Ask each companion one question they have never been asked.
Eat in comfortable silence. Let the quiet do the work.
Plan tomorrow's route with the group.

BOND_STAR.title: WATCHING THE MOONS
prose: *Camp — Late Night*

Solinari is full tonight, silver-white. Lunitari hangs low on the horizon, red as old copper. Between them, if you know where to look: the absence that is Nuitari, the black moon. Three moons. Three kinds of power. Three ways of seeing the same sky.

You are not alone on the watch. Someone has joined you without asking, which is the specific trust of people who have stopped requiring invitations. You sit together in the silver-red dark and look at the moons and do not speak for a while.
Thessaly, if present, writes in her notebook by moonlight. Tariel, if present, watches the moons the way she watches everything — cataloguing. Lysander, if present, looks south.

boxes.text: The three moons hang above the camp, silver and distant. You feel the divine presence — faint, patient, unsurprised by any of this. Mark 1 tally under whichever god feels right.

choices.text: 'Do you believe in the gods?'
'What are you fighting for? Not the Order. You, personally.'
Say nothing. Some moments do not need words.
Point out Nuitari. Not everyone can see the black moon.

BOND_RETURN.title: THE ROAD CONTINUES
prose: The moment passes. Tomorrow continues. But something has shifted — the invisible architecture of the group has settled into a new shape, one where you are a little more known and a little more trusted. That is not nothing. In a war, it is almost everything.

choices.text: Continue.

4A.title: THE MEASURE AND THE MORNING
prose: Aldous closes the book slowly — not because he is finished, but because some conversations end by settling rather than stopping. He places one hand flat on the cover, a gesture so habitual it has worn the leather smooth.

'You ask the right questions,' he says. 'That is rarer than you think. Half the people in this garrison have never asked a single one.' He looks at you with the particular quality of attention he reserves for moments he considers important. 'The Trials will test whether you have memorised the Measure. They will not test whether you understand it. Those are different examinations, and only one of them matters.'

He stands, joints protesting in the way of a man who has spent too many years in cold stone rooms. 'Go. The morning is yours. Spend it on something that matters — not because I told you to, but because you chose to.' He pauses at the door. 'That is the Measure, by the way. Choosing well, when no one is watching to see whether you do.'
The library is quiet after he leaves. The book is still on the table. The morning light through the narrow window has shifted from grey to gold.

choices.text: Step into the corridor. The morning is waiting.

BOND_HUB.title: CAMP
prose: The group settles. The road awaits.

choices.text: Return to camp preparations.
Continue the march south.

DATE_POLY.title: THE THREE OF YOU
prose: *Camp — Evening*

It takes time to learn the geometry of three. Not the logistics — those are simpler than expected — but the emotional architecture: who needs attention when, whose silence means contentment and whose means distance, how to hold two people in your awareness at once without making either feel secondary.

Tonight the geometry works. You are together — all three — at the edge of the firelight. They talked to each other first, before either of them talked to you. That matters. This is not two separate relationships with you at the centre. It is a triangle, and tonight all three sides are holding.

boxes.text:
**POLYAMORY SCENE**
A moment with both partners. Only available if the polyamory path was accepted.

choices.text: Ask them: 'How are you two?' Not about you. About them.
Let the evening unfold. No agenda. Just presence.
'I never expected this. I am glad it is real.'

DATE_POLY_MORN.title: MORNING AFTER
prose: *Camp — Dawn*

You wake to the specific warmth of not being alone. The morning has the quality of something new that is also, somehow, already familiar. One of them is still asleep. The other is watching you with the quiet expression of someone who has been awake for a while and decided not to move because the moment was worth holding.
'Good morning,' they say. Not a question. A fact they are pleased about.

boxes.text:
**POLYAMORY SCENE**
The morning after. Tender. Real.

choices.text: 'Good morning.' Mean everything.
Let the sleeping one sleep. Talk quietly with the other.

DISGRACE.title: REMOVED FROM THE ORDER
prose: The summons arrives without ceremony. A single page, unsmiling, hands you a sealed letter bearing the sigil of the Grand Master.
You already know what it says.
The words blur as you read them. *Conduct unbecoming. Failure to uphold the Measure. Effective immediately, you are stripped of rank, title, and the right to bear the arms of the Solamnic Orders.*
Your tabard is taken. Your shield, with its fresh-painted device, is turned face-down on the armoury table. The other squires do not meet your eyes.
Ser Aldous finds you at the gate. He does not speak for a long time.
"The Measure is not a suggestion, Squire. It is what separates us from the darkness we fight against. You forgot that."
He places a hand on your shoulder — briefly, as if even that much costs him something.
"Go. Find your honour again. The Orders will be here when you do."
You walk out through the gates of Castle Brightholm for the last time. The road stretches south. You carry nothing but your name, and even that feels lighter than it should.
*But the road is long, and redemption is not given — it is earned. In the shadow of the Dragon Queen, perhaps a disgraced squire can find what was lost.*

1A.title: THE BELL
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Squires' Barracks — Dawn*

The bell finds you the way it always does — not gently. It splits the dark like an axe through kindling, three sharp peals that bounce off the stone walls of the barracks and settle into your bones. Around you, the familiar sounds of morning: boots hitting flagstone, buckles catching, the low cursing of squires who will never be morning people no matter how many dawns the Order throws at them. You swing your legs over the side of your bunk and reach for your gambeson. Seven days until the Trials. The thought sits in your chest like a stone you swallowed in your sleep.

Aldric Voss is already dressed. Of course he is. His armour is laid out on his footlocker in the same pattern it has occupied every morning for two years — gorget, then vambraces, then greaves, left before right, each piece wiped down and checked for rust before it goes on. He does not rush. He does not dawdle. He moves like a man following a map only he can see. Across the aisle, Vael Ashmore's bunk is empty, sheets pulled tight enough to bounce a coin off, corners tucked with geometric precision. She's been up for an hour at least. You can hear her footsteps in the corridor — she runs the inner wall before dawn, every day, rain or frost or festival.

You pull your tabard over your head and feel the weight of the Solamnic crest settle across your shoulders. The kingfisher and crown, stitched in thread that was once silver and is now the colour of old dishwater. It smells like every squire who wore it before you — iron and sweat and something faintly like hope. The barracks empties around you in the old rhythm, the current that carries you all forward: dress, check, form up. The same as yesterday. The same as tomorrow. The ordinary machinery of the knighthood, turning.

choices.text: Head to the mess hall. Breakfast first.
Go straight to the courtyard — you're not hungry.
Check the notice board on your way out.

1B.title: THE MESS HALL
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Great Hall — Morning*

The mess hall is noise and steam and the smell of porridge that has been cooking since the fourth watch. Long trestle tables run the length of the room, scarred with knife marks and the carved initials of squires who came before you — some of whom are knights now, some of whom are dead, and some of whom went home and never spoke of this place again. You take your place on the bench and accept your bowl. The porridge is grey. The bread is yesterday's. The ale is weak enough to insult a child. You eat it all, because you have learned that a knight who skips a meal is a knight who falls in the afternoon.

At the far table, two senior knights sit close together, voices low. Ser Darrath — the tall one with the burn scar — gestures dismissively with a chunk of bread. 'Peasant hysteria,' he says, loud enough that he means you to hear it. 'Every harvest season some farmer sees smoke and screams Dragon Army.' The knight across from him — Ser Velthan, who walks with a limp from a border skirmish no one talks about — says nothing. He turns his mug in his hands. Around you, the other squires trade the same rumours they've been trading for a week: refugees at the south gate, smoke on the eastern horizon visible from the watchtower at dawn, a supply caravan two days overdue.

Ser Velthan stands. He has not finished his meal. He leaves his plate and walks out without a word, and you notice — or think you notice — that his hand brushes the hilt of his sword as he passes through the door. Not drawing. Checking. The way you check that something is still there.

boxes.text:
**INSIGHT CHECK:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+0) | WIS | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** You catch it — Ser Velthan's eyes, just before he stood. Not dismissive. Not calm. Afraid. And under his bench, half-hidden by his cloak: a packed campaign bag, bedroll cinched tight, rations visible at the top. He's ready to ride. He's been ready.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** The knights' argument seems routine. Senior officers always bicker about intelligence reports. You turn back to your porridge.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Sit with the other squires and listen. Rumours have roots.
Approach Ser Velthan outside. Ask him directly what he knows.
Find Thessaly — she'll know more than anyone in this room.
Eat quickly and get to training. Rumours won't sharpen your sword.

1C.title: THE NOTICE BOARD
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Main Corridor — Morning*

The notice board is the castle's memory, pinned and layered and slowly buried under its own weight. Duty rosters in Ser Brennan's blunt handwriting — you find your name on the afternoon stable detail. Supply requisitions, most of them weeks old, their edges curling. A formal posting of the Trials schedule: seven days, three tests, no exceptions, no delays. Someone has drawn a small skull in the margin next to the Trial of the Sword. You suspect Vael.

Three things catch your eye. First: a press credential, stamped with the seal of the Palanthas Herald, pinned neatly beside the duty roster. 'Thessaly Brightwater — War Correspondent, authorized by Lord Caeron Ashvale.' Beneath it, someone has scrawled in charcoal: *Why is a journalist in a military castle?* No one has answered. Second: a requisition for healing herbs — silverleaf, feverfew, and dreamless-sleep root — signed in careful letters by Mira of the Oaks, addressed to the infirmary. The quantities are large. Larger than a castle infirmary should need for peacetime. Third: a wanted sketch, partially torn. What remains shows a face — hard-jawed, cropped hair, a scar through the left eyebrow. Beneath it, fragments of text: '...deserted the Red Wing...information regarding...approach with caution...' The rest is gone, ripped away.

You run your thumb along the tear. The paper is thick, military stock, and the tear is clean — a straight pull from the bottom corner, not the ragged fraying of age or weather. Someone removed the rest of this poster. Recently.

boxes.text:
**PERCEPTION CHECK:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+0) | WIS | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** The tear is deliberate — cut, not torn, with something sharp. And there's a thumbtack hole at the top where a second copy was pinned. That one is gone entirely. Someone is removing these.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** The poster is damaged, but you can't tell whether it was deliberate or just the usual wear of a busy corridor.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Head to training. The morning is wasting.
Ask around about the deserter poster. Someone knows something.
Follow up on the herb requisition — visit the infirmary.

TR1.title: SHIELD WALL DRILL
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Training Yard — Mid-Morning*

The training yard is a rectangle of packed earth bordered by stone walls that have absorbed three centuries of impact. Ser Brennan stands at its centre with his arms folded and his voice pitched to carry. He is not tall, but he fills space the way a boulder fills a river — everything moves around him. 'Shields up. Lock formation. You are not individuals. You are a wall. Walls do not think. Walls do not flinch. Walls hold.' Twenty squires shoulder together, round shields overlapping, and you feel the formation click into place like a key turning in a lock. Aldric is on your right. His shield-edge meets yours with a thud that travels up your arm and into your shoulder. He does not look at you. He does not need to. You have done this a hundred times. His weight is steady, his breathing even, and you know — the way you know your own heartbeat — that he will not give ground.

Across the formation, Vael anchors the opposite wing. She is smaller than most of the squires around her, and it does not matter. Her stance is low, her centre of gravity planted like a tree root, and when the line shifts she absorbs it without moving her feet. Brennan nods once — not at anyone, at the wall itself — and raises his hand. The senior squires line up opposite, wooden training swords drawn, faces grim with the particular enthusiasm of older students asked to hit younger ones legally.

'Brace!' Brennan's hand drops. The charge hits the wall like a wave hitting a cliff, and for a moment the world is nothing but pressure and noise — the crack of wood on shields, boots scraping for purchase, someone grunting, someone swearing. Aldric's shoulder presses into yours. The wall bows. The wall holds. Or it doesn't.

boxes.text:
**ATHLETICS CHECK:** Athletics (STR, proficiency if trained) (+0) | STR | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** The wall holds at your position. You absorb the impact through your legs and push back, and the senior squire opposite you slides half a step. Brennan's eyes find you — a nod, small and hard, worth more than a week of praise. You'll carry that steady stance into your next shield fight.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** The wall buckles. Your foot slips and the senior squire drives through, and for a moment the whole formation staggers sideways because of you. Brennan watches. He says nothing. He writes something on his slate. The silence is worse than any reprimand.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Ask Brennan for extra drill time after formation.
Help the squire next to you who stumbled — she's trying not to show it.
Challenge Aldric to a private shield bout.

TR2.title: THE SWORD FORMS
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Inner Courtyard — Late Morning*

The inner courtyard is yours alone. The other squires have scattered to their own corners of the castle for solo practice, and you have claimed the square of sunlight between the chapel buttress and the armoury wall — the spot with the best footing, the one everyone wants. Your sword is a training blade, blunted steel with a leather grip worn smooth by a dozen hands before yours, but it balances well and it knows the forms as well as you do. You begin.

Vigilance: high guard, eyes forward, blade angled to catch the light and the enemy's gaze. Temperance: the controlled retreat, giving ground without giving advantage. Mercy: the blade turned, the flat presented, the strike that subdues without killing. Resolve: feet planted, sword driven forward in a thrust that commits everything. Patience: the wait, blade low, breathing steady, letting the opponent come to you. Sacrifice: the opening you leave deliberately, the wound you accept to create a killing stroke. Justice: the final form, the overhead cut that ends it — clean, total, absolute. You have done them a thousand times. Today, in the morning light that turns your blade into a line of white fire, one of them feels different. Heavier. More real. As if the virtue it was named for has weight, and you are only now strong enough to feel it.

boxes.text: **THE SEVEN FORMS:** Each form of the Solamnic sword is named for a virtue of the knighthood. In the old days, it was said a knight who mastered all seven had mastered themselves. Today they are muscle memory. But muscle memory is a kind of knowing.

choices.text: Mercy feels different today. You linger on it — the turned blade, the choice not to cut.
Resolve. You drive the form harder than the others, again and again until your arm shakes.
Justice. The last form. You hold it until your arm burns and your shadow stretches long.

TR3.title: MOUNTED COMBAT
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Stable Yard — Afternoon*

Valor knows you before you reach her stall. She turns her grey head and pushes her nose into your chest with the blunt affection of an animal that has decided you belong to her. You cinch the saddle, check the girth twice — Brennan's voice in your head, *a loose girth kills more squires than lances* — and lead her into the stable yard where the quintains are set and the riding lanes marked with chalk. The afternoon sun is warm on your shoulders. Valor's hooves are steady on the packed earth. She has done this as many times as you have, and she is better at it.

The drill is simple: lance work at the trot, wheeling turns at the canter, then the full charge — three passes at the quintain, hit the target, duck the counterweight, control the horse. You settle the lance in the crook of your arm and feel its weight find the familiar groove. Valor gathers beneath you. The first pass is clean. The second is better. On the third, you hear it — a crack, a cry, the heavy sound of a body hitting ground. Across the yard, a young squire — you don't know her name, she arrived last month — has come off her horse during the charge. The horse bolts. The squire is on the ground, trying to push herself up, one arm cradled against her chest. The drill continues around her. Brennan hasn't called a halt. The other riders wheel past, eyes forward.
Valor's ears flatten. She feels the loose horse, the disruption in the pattern, and her stride shortens. She wants direction. The fallen squire gets one knee under her and fails to stand.

boxes.text:
**ANIMAL HANDLING CHECK:** Animal Handling (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+0) | WIS | DC 11
**SUCCESS (roll 11+):** You bring Valor to a smooth stop with a word and a shift of weight. She trusts you. You dismount cleanly, reins looped, lance stowed — textbook.
**FAILURE (roll 1-10):** Valor shies at the loose horse, sidestepping hard, and you lose a stirrup. By the time you have her under control, precious seconds have passed.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Break formation to help the fallen squire. The drill can wait.
Complete the drill first, then check on her. Discipline holds.
Call for Mira — the infirmary is closest.
Finish your pass, then ride to the fallen squire at speed — fastest response that maintains form.

OA1.title: RECITATION
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Great Hall — Morning Assembly*

The great hall holds all of you — squires, sergeants, the handful of full knights stationed at Brightholm — and still feels half-empty. It was built for a garrison three times this size, in an age when the knighthood filled castles the way rivers fill valleys: naturally, inevitably, without effort. Now the empty spaces echo. Lord Caeron Ashvale stands on the dais in full ceremonial plate, the crest of the Rose on his surcoat, his face carved from the same stone as the walls. He does not speak. He waits. The hall falls silent the way a held breath falls silent — not because the noise stopped, but because something replaced it.

You stand with the other squires and speak the words together. '*Est Sularus oth Mithas.*' My honour is my life. The Old Solamnic rolls off your tongue with the ease of long practice, and the Common translation follows like its shadow. The words hit the far wall and come back changed, layered, multiplied — fifty voices made into one voice, one voice made into something that sounds, for a moment, like certainty. You notice who stumbles. A squire near the back loses the cadence and mouths the rest. You notice who means it. Aldric speaks the Oath the way he does everything — precisely, completely, as if each syllable is a brick and he is building something that must not fall. Vael's lips move quickly, efficiently, her eyes already on the door. And Lysander Graves, the quiet soldier who carries more than his share and speaks less than his silence — Lysander mouths the words without sound. His lips move. No voice comes.
Lord Caeron lets the echo die. Then he says, 'Dismissed,' and the hall exhales.

boxes.text:
**RELIGION CHECK:** Religion (INT, proficiency if trained) (+0) | INT | DC 10
**SUCCESS (roll 10+):** It strikes you between one breath and the next. *My honour is my life.* You'd always heard it as pride — my honour is so great it defines me. But the old Solamnic is conditional, not declarative. If my honour dies, I die. It is not a boast. It is a warning. The knights who wrote those words meant them the way a doctor means *this wound is fatal.* As diagnosis. As fact.
**FAILURE (roll 1-9):** The words wash over you the way they always do — familiar, steady, comforting. You feel their weight but cannot name it.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Find Ser Aldous after the recitation. You have questions about the Oath.
The recitation settles you. Head to morning duties with clear purpose.
Catch up with Lysander — he didn't speak the words. Why?

OA2.title: THE MEASURE'S WEIGHT
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Chapel Library — Afternoon*

Ser Aldous is where he always is — in the chapel library, surrounded by books that are older than the castle and twice as stubborn. Afternoon light falls through the narrow windows in pale slabs, catching the dust that rises when he turns a page. He is not a large man. He is not an imposing man. He is the kind of man you forget is in the room until he speaks, and then you wonder how you ever missed him. He looks up when you enter and gestures to the chair opposite. It is the only other chair in the room. He has been expecting someone.

'Sit,' he says. 'I want to tell you about Ser Gavren of the Rose.' He opens a ledger — handwritten, the ink faded to brown — and turns it so you can see. 'Three hundred years ago, during the second campaign against the Ergothian border lords, Ser Gavren was ordered to hold a bridge over the Vingaard. A company of sixty against an advancing force of four hundred. The Measure was explicit: hold until relieved. Relief was dispatched. Relief was ambushed on the road and turned back. No one told Gavren.' Aldous traces a line of text with his finger. 'He held for two days. On the third day, with eleven men remaining, the enemy crossed the river upstream and flanked him. He died on the bridge. The villages behind him — three of them, perhaps a thousand souls — had time to evacuate. But Gavren could not have known that. He held because the Measure said hold.'

Aldous closes the ledger. 'The question I put to every squire before the Trials is this: was he right? The Measure commanded him to hold. His own judgement might have said retreat — save himself, save his men, fight again another day. The Measure is not a suggestion. It is law. But it was written by mortals, in a world that has since been broken and remade by gods.' He folds his hands. 'I am not asking for the right answer. I am asking for yours.'

boxes.text: **THE MEASURE'S WEIGHT:** The Solamnic code is not a suggestion. It is law. Knights who break it are not heroes — they are oathbreakers. But the Measure was written by mortals in a world that has since been shattered by gods. The question is not whether the Measure is right. The question is whether you can hold it when holding costs everything.

choices.text: 'He was right to hold. The Oath demands it, and the Oath is all we have.'
'He should have retreated. A dead knight saves no one tomorrow.'
'He was right — but the system that left him without relief was wrong.'
'I don't know. And I think that's the honest answer.'

OA3.title: THE TRIAL PREVIEW
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Chapel Library — Late Afternoon*

Ser Aldous walks you through it with the calm of a man describing the weather. Three tests. Three Orders. The Trial of the Crown: you will be given an order. It will be clear, it will be lawful, and it will cost you something to follow. Obedience is not the absence of doubt — it is action in the presence of doubt. The Trial of the Sword: you will fight. Not a sparring match, not a contest. A fight where standing your ground matters more than winning, where the test is not whether you can kill but whether you can refuse to run. The Trial of the Rose: you will be asked to sacrifice something. Not your life — that would be easy. Something smaller, something that matters only to you, something no one will see you give up.

He pauses at the chapel window. The light is failing, and his shadow stretches across the stone floor like a long road. 'You will fail at least one,' he says. 'Every squire does. I failed the Rose. Caeron failed the Sword, though he'd cut out my tongue if he knew I told you.' A thin smile. 'What matters is not the failure. Any fool can succeed at a test designed for success. What matters is what you do in the moment after you fail — whether you stay down, or whether you get up and keep walking. The Trials do not make you a knight. They show you who you already are.'
He turns from the window. 'Seven days. Use them.'

boxes.text: **THE TRIALS:** Seven days remain. The Trials will test your body, your mind, and your character. You cannot prepare for all three equally. What you do with the remaining days will determine which test finds you ready — and which finds you wanting.

choices.text: Ask him which trial he failed — and what it taught him.
'Which test should I be most afraid of?'
Thank him and leave. You have work to do.

OA4.title: THE REFUGEE GATE
prose: *Castle Brightholm, South Gate — Late Afternoon*

You are carrying a message to the outer storehouse when you hear the voices. The south gate is narrow — built for defence, not welcome — and in its shadow stands a family of four. The father is doing the talking, hands open, palms up, the universal posture of a man who has nothing left to bargain with. He is lean and sun-dark and his voice has the careful steadiness of someone who has already shouted and learned that shouting does not work. The mother stands behind him with a child on her hip — the younger one, two or three, face buried in her shoulder. The older child, a girl of perhaps eight, stands apart from both parents. She is watching the gate. She is watching you.

Ser Halwynn, the duty officer, is patient in the way that procedure makes people patient. 'I understand your situation,' he says, and he probably does. 'But Castle Brightholm is a military installation. I cannot admit civilians without authorization from the commanding officer or evidence of immediate threat to life. Article Seventeen.' He recites it without checking the book. He has said this before. Recently. The father begins to argue — they've walked for days, the children need rest, there is nowhere else — and Halwynn listens, because listening is part of the procedure too.

The older child's eyes find the crest on your tabard. The kingfisher and crown. To her, that crest means something. To her, you are not a squire running an errand. You are a knight. You are the promise the knighthood made, a long time ago, to people exactly like her. She does not speak. She does not need to.

boxes.text:
**INSIGHT CHECK:** Insight (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+0) | WIS | DC 12
**SUCCESS (roll 12+):** The father's boots are worn through — not at the sole, but at the sides, where leather gives way on rocky ground. Three days' walk from the nearest village would not do that. These boots have crossed hills, fords, broken terrain. This family has been walking for a week or more. They didn't leave a village. They left a place where a village used to be.
**FAILURE (roll 1-11):** The family looks tired and road-worn, but you cannot read more from their condition. Many travellers look this way after days on the road.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

**THE MEASURE SAYS:** Article Seventeen of the Garrison Code: 'No civilian shall be admitted to a military installation without authorization from the commanding officer or evidence of immediate threat to life.' The family is tired. They are not in immediate danger. The Measure is clear. The child is still watching you.

choices.text: You are a squire. You have no authority here. Walk on.
Speak to Ser Halwynn privately — ask if there's a way within the Measure to help.
Give the family your rations. You can skip dinner.
Remember this. Say nothing. Carry it.

EV1.title: THE CAMPFIRE
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Barracks Yard — Evening*

The fire is small and the night is large. Someone built it from scraps — broken lance shafts, a fence post that no one will miss, kindling stuffed with dried grass — and it burns the way all campfires burn: unevenly, generously, with a warmth that reaches further than it should. The squires gather in loose clusters, sitting on upturned crates and rolled blankets, and for a little while the Trials and the rumours and the smoke on the horizon are just stories. One squire — Gareth, from the northern coast — tells the Cataclysm legend the way his grandmother told it: the Kingpriest who demanded godhood, and the gods who answered with a mountain of fire that split the world in half. Another talks about her family's vineyard, the harvest she's missing, the way the light falls through the grape leaves in autumn. The laughter comes easy. The silences come easier.

Sera Nighthollow sits at the fire's edge, outside the circle, a whetstone moving along her knife blade in a rhythm that never varies. She is listening to everything and participating in nothing. Her eyes move from face to face with the steady attention of someone cataloguing exits. Beyond her, barely in the firelight, a figure sits with three books open across their knees — Tariel Duskmantle, scholar, their face lit from below by the fire and from above by a stub of candle wedged into a crack in the wall. They are reading about something that happened a thousand years ago as if it might happen again tomorrow. And in the deepest shadow, leaning against the barracks wall with his arms folded: Lysander Graves. He watches the yard behind the fire — the dark part, where the light doesn't reach — the way a man watches a door he expects to open.

boxes.text:
**COMPANION AVAILABLE: TARIEL DUSKMANTLE**
Scholar of pre-Cataclysm history. Quiet, intense, reads constantly. Carries more books than weapons. Knows things no one else remembers — or wants to.

**COMPANION AVAILABLE: LYSANDER GRAVES**
Quiet. Carries more than his share. Fights like someone who has done this before. Does not talk about before. Watches the rear guard like someone who has been ambushed.

choices.text: Sit with Tariel. Ask what they're reading.
Approach Lysander. He's been alone all evening.
Stay with the group. Listen to the stories.
Join Sera at the edge. She hears things.

EV2.title: THE WATCHTOWER
prose: *Castle Brightholm, East Watchtower — Night*

The watchtower stairs are worn smooth in the centre, cupped by centuries of boots, and the wind hits you as you clear the last turn — a steady eastern blow that smells of distance and something else, something acrid, like the memory of smoke. The watch sergeant, a man named Corben who has held this post for nine years and sleeps in four-hour shifts like a dog, grunts when you arrive. 'On time,' he says. 'That's one for you.' He hands you his spyglass — brass, dented, the lens cracked along one edge — and points east. 'Tell me what you see.'

You see the horizon. You see the dark shapes of the Kharolis foothills, black against a sky that is not quite black. You see stars — the constellations of Paladine and Takhisis wheeling in their eternal opposition — and beneath them, low and steady and wrong, a glow. Orange. Not the orange of a campfire, which flickers and breathes. This is constant, a stain on the horizon's edge, and it is brighter than it was yesterday. You know this because Corben has drawn a mark on the tower's stone parapet — a line of charcoal, renewed each night, showing where the glow meets the hills. The line has moved.
'Three nights now,' Corben says. He does not look at the glow. He looks at you looking at it. 'Getting closer. Or getting bigger. Hard to tell which is worse.'

boxes.text:
**PERCEPTION CHECK:** Perception (WIS, proficiency if trained) (+0) | WIS | DC 13
**SUCCESS (roll 13+):** You brace the spyglass against the parapet and steady your breathing. The glow resolves — not one fire but many, spread along a line. A burning town, maybe two. And the line is moving, which means the burning is moving, which means someone is setting the fires as they advance. Three days' march. Maybe two. Whatever is out there is large, organized, and coming this way.
**FAILURE (roll 1-12):** You can see the glow but cannot judge its distance or nature through the cracked lens. It could be a forest fire. It could be a burning settlement. It could be anything. But Corben's charcoal line does not lie. It is closer tonight than it was last night.
*No dice? Flip a coin: Heads = pass | Tails = fail*

choices.text: Report it to Lord Caeron in the morning. Someone with authority needs to know.
Tell Thessaly. She'll investigate — and she'll get the word out.
Tell no one yet. Watch it again tomorrow. Confirm before you alarm anyone.
Ask Corben what he thinks it is.

EV3.title: LETTERS HOME
prose: *Castle Brightholm, Squires' Barracks — Late Night*

The barracks settles into its nighttime quiet — not silence, never silence, but the lowered volume of people trying to be still. Candle stubs gutter in clay dishes. The scratching of quills on parchment fills the spaces between breathing, a sound like mice in the walls, like small determined creatures building something in the dark. Tomorrow's duties are posted. The Trials are six days away. Tonight, the squires write home.

Vael writes three pages in tight, efficient script, her pen moving without pause or correction — she knows exactly what she wants to say and she says it exactly once. She folds the letter into thirds, seals it with a plain wax stamp, and is asleep within minutes. Aldric sits on his bunk with a sheet of paper on his knee and a pen in his hand. He writes nothing. He stares at the blank page as if it contains a question he cannot answer, then folds it — still blank — and slides it under his pillow. In the officers' alcove, visible through the half-open door, Lord Caeron sits at his desk with a single candle and an empty page. His family signet ring — the Ashvale hawk — is turned inward on his finger, pressed against his palm, hidden. He does not write either. Lysander Graves has no paper, no pen, no candle. He lies on his bunk with his boots still on, arms folded across his chest, eyes open, staring at the ceiling as if it might open.
The candles burn down. The wind moves against the shutters. Somewhere beyond the walls, the glow on the eastern horizon brightens by a degree that no one is awake to measure.

choices.text: Write home. Tell them you're ready. Whether or not it's true.
Write home. Tell them the truth — you're afraid, and that's all right.
Don't write. Words won't help. Sleep instead.
Sit with Aldric. He doesn't need to talk. Neither do you.
